questions.theory: When class frequencies are skewed, accuracy is dominated by the majority class. F1, the harmonic mean of precision and recall, focuses on minority-class performance and balances the two error types, making it a common choice under imbalance.

questions.question: Performing feature selection using the full dataset (including test data) before cross-validation leads to optimistic bias because:
options: It reduces the number of features too aggressively
Cross-validation cannot be run afterward
It always selects irrelevant features
The selected features are chosen partly based on test-set signal, leaking information into the model
questions.explanation: Selecting features with knowledge of the test labels lets test information influence the model, inflating estimated performance.
questions.theory: Feature selection is part of model fitting and must occur inside each cross-validation fold using only training data. Selecting features on the whole dataset leaks target information from the held-out folds, producing overly optimistic error estimates.

questions.question: Stratified k-fold cross-validation is particularly important for imbalanced classification because it:
options: Preserves the class proportions in every fold, giving stable and representative estimates
Guarantees each fold has zero minority examples
Removes the need for a test set
Eliminates variance in model predictions
questions.explanation: Stratification keeps each fold's class ratio close to the overall ratio, preventing folds with too few or zero minority examples.
questions.theory: Plain k-fold can produce folds with very few minority instances under imbalance, yielding noisy or undefined metrics. Stratified k-fold samples each fold to mirror the global class distribution, improving the reliability of cross-validated estimates.

questions.question: Adding more training data is generally more effective at reducing variance (overfitting) than at reducing bias (underfitting).
options: True
False
questions.explanation: More data constrains a flexible model and reduces variance, but it cannot fix bias from a model too simple to represent the true function.
questions.theory: Variance stems from sensitivity to the particular training sample, which shrinks as the sample grows. Bias stems from the model's inability to represent the target; only changing the model class or capacity reduces it, not additional data alone.

questions.question: Elastic net regularization combines L1 and L2 penalties. A key advantage over pure lasso (L1) is that elastic net:
options: Cannot perform feature selection
Always produces denser models than L2
Eliminates the need for any hyperparameter tuning
Handles groups of correlated features more gracefully, tending to select them together
questions.explanation: The L2 component encourages correlated predictors to be selected and shrunk together, avoiding lasso's tendency to arbitrarily pick one.
questions.theory: Pure lasso may select only one feature from a correlated group and drop the rest unstably. Elastic net adds an L2 term that promotes a grouping effect, retaining sparsity while stabilizing selection among correlated variables.

questions.question: What does it mean for an estimator to have high variance in the bias-variance framework?
options: Its irreducible error is large
Its predictions change substantially when trained on different samples from the same distribution
Its predictions are systematically far from the true value regardless of training data
It always underfits the data
questions.explanation: High variance means the learned function is highly sensitive to which particular training set was drawn, producing unstable predictions.
questions.theory: Variance measures how much the model's predictions fluctuate across different training samples drawn from the same distribution. High-variance models overfit, capturing sample-specific noise; bias measures the systematic error of the average prediction.

questions.question: For a convex loss function with a single global minimum, what is true about gradient descent with a sufficiently small fixed learning rate?
options: It may converge to a local minimum that is not global
It cannot make progress without momentum
It is guaranteed to converge toward the global minimum
It requires second-order derivatives to converge
questions.explanation: On a convex surface there are no non-global local minima, so a small enough step size drives gradient descent to the global optimum.
questions.theory: Convex functions have a single global minimum and no spurious local minima. With an appropriately small learning rate, gradient descent decreases the objective monotonically and converges to the global minimizer; non-convex losses lack this guarantee.

questions.question: Why might undersampling the majority class to balance a dataset be undesirable?
options: It discards potentially useful majority-class information, increasing variance
It always causes data leakage
It synthesizes unrealistic minority examples
It makes the classes more imbalanced
questions.explanation: Removing majority examples throws away real data, which can raise variance and degrade the model's view of the majority class.
questions.theory: Undersampling rebalances classes by deleting majority instances, which is simple but wastes data. Oversampling, SMOTE, or class weighting retain all information; the right choice depends on dataset size and the cost of discarding data.

questions.question: In the confusion-matrix definitions, specificity (true-negative rate) is computed as:
options: TN/(TN+FP)
TN/(TN+FN)
TP/(TP+FN)
TP/(TP+FP)
questions.explanation: Specificity is the fraction of actual negatives correctly identified, TN divided by all true negatives plus false positives.
questions.theory: Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) measures how well a classifier avoids false alarms among true negatives, complementing recall (sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN)). The ROC curve plots sensitivity against 1 minus specificity (the false-positive rate).

questions.question: A model is trained to predict customer churn, and one input feature is 'account_closed_flag.' Including this feature likely causes:
options: Target leakage, since the flag is essentially a proxy for the outcome being predicted
Underfitting due to too few features
No effect on model validity
Reduced training accuracy
questions.explanation: A feature that directly encodes or results from the target gives the model the answer, producing unrealistically high performance that collapses in deployment.
questions.theory: Target leakage occurs when a feature contains information about the label that would not be available at prediction time. Such features inflate offline metrics but fail in production; careful audit of feature provenance and timing prevents this.

questions.question: Why does averaging the predictions of many independently trained high-variance models reduce overall variance?
options: Averaging removes the need for training data
It converts variance into irreducible error
If errors are uncorrelated, the variance of the average shrinks roughly in proportion to the number of models
Averaging increases each model's bias to compensate
questions.explanation: For uncorrelated errors, the variance of an average of N models is about 1/N of an individual model's variance.
questions.theory: The variance of the mean of N i.i.d. predictions equals sigma^2/N. Ensemble methods like bagging exploit this: combining diverse, decorrelated models reduces variance without raising bias, improving generalization. Residual correlation limits the gain.

questions.question: Which statement about AdaBoost is correct?
options: It requires deep, fully grown trees as weak learners
It trains all weak learners on identical, equally weighted data
It reduces variance but never bias
It increases the weights of misclassified examples so subsequent learners focus on them
questions.explanation: AdaBoost reweights the training distribution after each round to emphasize previously misclassified points, then combines learners by weighted vote.
questions.theory: AdaBoost is a boosting algorithm that maintains example weights, increasing them for misclassified instances each iteration so later weak learners (often shallow stumps) concentrate on hard cases. Final predictions are a weighted combination based on each learner's accuracy.

questions.question: When the cost of a false negative greatly exceeds the cost of a false positive, you should generally:
options: Remove the minority class from training
Use accuracy as the sole metric
Raise the classification threshold to increase precision
Lower the classification threshold to increase recall
questions.explanation: Lowering the threshold catches more true positives (higher recall), reducing costly false negatives at the expense of more false positives.
questions.theory: Threshold selection should reflect asymmetric error costs. If missing a positive (false negative) is far worse, favor recall by lowering the threshold; cost-sensitive analysis or the precision-recall tradeoff guides the optimal operating point.

questions.question: The regularization strength hyperparameter (often denoted lambda) in ridge regression controls:
options: The number of training epochs
The learning rate of gradient descent
The number of features in the dataset
The tradeoff between fitting the training data and keeping weights small
questions.explanation: Lambda scales the penalty term, balancing data-fit loss against the magnitude of the weights and thereby tuning bias versus variance.
questions.theory: Ridge minimizes loss plus lambda times the squared weight norm. Larger lambda shrinks weights more, raising bias and lowering variance; smaller lambda fits data more closely. Lambda is typically chosen by cross-validation to optimize generalization.

questions.question: A perfect ROC-AUC of 1.0 on the test set, combined with mediocre accuracy at the default 0.5 threshold, can occur if the classifier ranks examples perfectly but is poorly calibrated.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: AUC depends only on ranking, so perfect separation yields AUC 1.0 even if predicted probabilities are miscalibrated, making the 0.5 threshold suboptimal.
questions.theory: ROC-AUC measures ranking quality, not calibration. A model can order all positives above all negatives (AUC = 1) yet output probabilities concentrated such that 0.5 is a bad cutoff. Threshold tuning or calibration fixes the accuracy gap.

questions.question: Why are shallow decision trees (stumps) commonly used as base learners in boosting, whereas deep trees are common in bagging?
options: Shallow trees are faster to visualize
Bagging cannot use trees at all
Boosting needs high-bias, low-variance learners that it incrementally combines, while bagging needs high-variance learners to average down
Deep trees cannot be boosted
questions.explanation: Boosting reduces bias by sequentially combining many weak (high-bias) learners, while bagging reduces variance by averaging strong (high-variance) learners.
questions.theory: Boosting builds an additive model from weak learners, so simple high-bias stumps suffice and resist overfitting per round. Bagging averages independent models to cut variance, so it benefits from low-bias, high-variance learners like fully grown trees.

questions.question: Macro-averaged F1 across classes differs from micro-averaged F1 in that macro-averaging:
options: Weights each class by its number of examples
Ignores the minority classes
Weights each class equally regardless of its support
Is identical to overall accuracy
questions.explanation: Macro-F1 computes F1 per class and averages them with equal weight, so rare classes count as much as frequent ones.
questions.theory: Macro-averaging treats all classes equally by averaging per-class metrics, highlighting performance on rare classes. Micro-averaging aggregates global true positives, false positives, and false negatives, letting frequent classes dominate and approximating accuracy in single-label settings.

questions.question: In grouped data (e.g., multiple records per patient), failing to keep all of a group's records in the same fold causes:
options: Class imbalance
Underfitting
Leakage, because near-duplicate records from the same group appear in both train and validation sets
Vanishing gradients
questions.explanation: Splitting a group across folds lets the model see correlated information from the same entity during both training and evaluation, inflating performance.
questions.theory: When records share a grouping (patient, user, session), they are correlated. Group-aware splitting (e.g., GroupKFold) ensures all of a group's data stays on one side of the split, preventing leakage and yielding realistic generalization estimates.

questions.question: The 'double descent' phenomenon challenges the classical bias-variance picture by showing that:
options: Regularization has no effect on generalization
Test error can decrease again after the interpolation threshold as model capacity grows very large
Bias and variance are unrelated to capacity
Test error always increases monotonically with model size
questions.explanation: Beyond the point where the model exactly fits the training data, further increasing capacity can paradoxically lower test error again.
questions.theory: Classical theory predicts a U-shaped test-error curve in capacity. Double descent shows a second descent past the interpolation threshold: heavily overparameterized models can generalize well, a nuance important for understanding modern high-capacity learners.

questions.question: Why can a very high learning rate cause gradient descent to fail to converge?
options: It eliminates the need for a loss function
Large steps can overshoot the minimum and cause the loss to oscillate or diverge
It makes gradients undefined
It guarantees convergence to a saddle point
questions.explanation: Steps that are too large repeatedly jump past the minimum, so the loss bounces around or grows without settling.
questions.theory: The learning rate sets step size; if it exceeds a stability bound related to the loss curvature, updates overshoot the optimum and the objective may oscillate or diverge. Learning-rate schedules and adaptive methods help keep steps in a stable range.

questions.question: Mini-batch gradient descent is widely used because it:
options: Computes the exact full-dataset gradient each step
Requires no learning rate tuning
Only works for linear models
Balances the stability of batch gradients with the efficiency and noise benefits of stochastic updates
questions.explanation: Mini-batches give lower-variance gradient estimates than single-example SGD while remaining far cheaper than full-batch, and exploit vectorized hardware.
questions.theory: Mini-batch descent computes gradients over modest subsets, trading off the noisy cheap updates of SGD against the costly accurate updates of full-batch. It stabilizes training, leverages parallel hardware efficiently, and is the standard choice for large-scale learning.

questions.question: If a classifier's ROC-AUC is 0.5, its ranking ability is no better than random guessing.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: An AUC of 0.5 means a randomly chosen positive is equally likely to be ranked above or below a randomly chosen negative, indicating no discriminative power.
questions.theory: AUC equals the probability that the model ranks a random positive above a random negative. A value of 0.5 corresponds to the diagonal ROC line, i.e., chance-level discrimination; values below 0.5 indicate worse-than-random ranking that could be inverted.

questions.question: Resampling (oversampling or SMOTE) to fix class imbalance should be applied:
options: To the entire dataset before splitting into train and test
After computing the final evaluation metrics
To the test set only
Only to the training folds, after the train/validation split, to avoid leakage
questions.explanation: Resampling before splitting can place synthetic or duplicated copies of the same instance in both train and validation sets, leaking information and inflating scores.
questions.theory: Balancing techniques alter the training distribution and must be confined to training data within each cross-validation fold. Applying them before splitting (especially SMOTE, which interpolates) leaks information into evaluation, producing optimistic and unrealistic metrics.

questions.question: A model shows excellent cross-validation performance but fails badly in production. Which cause is LEAST likely to be a form of data leakage?
options: Preprocessing statistics computed on combined train and test data
Distribution shift between training data and live production data
A feature that is unavailable at prediction time in production
Target-derived features included during training
questions.explanation: Distribution shift is a mismatch between training and deployment data rather than leakage of information into the model during training.
questions.theory: Leakage means information from outside the legitimate training inputs (test statistics, future values, target-derived features) contaminates training, inflating offline metrics. Distribution shift is a distinct failure mode where the deployment distribution differs from training, degrading real-world performance without any leak.

questions.question: What is a neural network primarily inspired by?
options: A computer's hard drive
The structure of the human brain
The internet's network of cables
A spreadsheet of numbers
questions.explanation: Artificial neural networks are loosely inspired by how neurons in the brain connect and pass signals.
questions.theory: A neural network is a computing system loosely modeled on the brain, made of simple units called neurons connected together. The brain analogy is a starting point, though artificial networks are mathematical and far simpler than real biology.

questions.question: In a neural network, what is the basic building-block unit usually called?
options: A folder
A neuron (or node)
A cable
A pixel
questions.explanation: The fundamental processing unit of a neural network is called a neuron, also known as a node.
questions.theory: A neuron (node) is the smallest processing unit in a neural network. It takes in numbers, combines them, and produces an output that can be passed to other neurons.

questions.question: A neural network is made up of many simple units connected together.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Neural networks gain their power by combining many simple neurons into a connected structure.
questions.theory: Each individual neuron does something very simple, but connecting many of them lets the network represent complex patterns. This idea of simple parts working together is central to neural networks.

questions.question: What does the input layer of a neural network do?
options: Deletes unused neurons
Produces the final answer
Receives the data fed into the network
Stores the network on disk
questions.explanation: The input layer is where the raw data enters the network before being processed.
questions.theory: The input layer is the first layer of a neural network and holds the data you provide, such as the pixels of an image or the features of an example. It passes this data forward to the next layers.

questions.question: What does the output layer of a neural network produce?
options: A list of all neurons
The raw input data
The training dataset
The network's final result or prediction
questions.explanation: The output layer gives the network's final answer, such as a classification or a predicted value.
questions.theory: The output layer is the last layer of a neural network and produces the final result. For example, it might output the probability that an image is a cat.

questions.question: Layers that sit between the input and output layers are called what?
options: Backup layers
Final layers
Side layers
Hidden layers
questions.explanation: The layers between input and output are called hidden layers because they are not directly observed.
questions.theory: Hidden layers are the layers between the input and output. They are called hidden because we do not directly see their values as input or output, yet they do most of the internal processing.

questions.question: What is the typical order in which data flows through a basic neural network?
options: Output, then input, then hidden
Output, then hidden, then input
Hidden, then input, then output
Input, then hidden, then output
questions.explanation: Data enters at the input layer, passes through hidden layers, and emerges at the output layer.
questions.theory: In a standard feedforward neural network, information moves in one direction: from the input layer, through any hidden layers, to the output layer. This forward flow produces the network's prediction.

questions.question: What is a 'weight' in a neural network?
options: The number of layers in the network
The physical mass of the computer
How long training takes
A number that controls the strength of a connection
questions.explanation: A weight is a number that scales a connection, determining how strongly one neuron influences another.
questions.theory: Each connection between neurons has a weight, a number that controls how much influence one neuron's output has on the next. Larger weights mean a stronger effect on the receiving neuron.

questions.question: Neural networks learn by adjusting their weights.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Learning in a neural network mostly means gradually changing the weights to make better predictions.
questions.theory: When a neural network learns, it adjusts the weights on its connections so that its outputs get closer to the correct answers. This adjustment process is what we call training.

questions.question: What connects the neurons in a neural network?
options: Wireless signals
USB cables
Weighted connections
Magnets
questions.explanation: Neurons are linked by connections, each carrying a weight that scales the signal passed along it.
questions.theory: Neurons are joined by connections, and each connection has an associated weight. These weighted connections are how information and influence flow from one neuron to another.

questions.question: What is the role of an activation function in a neuron?
options: To store the training data
To decide the neuron's output based on its combined input
To count the number of layers
To draw the network diagram
questions.explanation: An activation function takes the neuron's combined input and transforms it into the neuron's output.
questions.theory: After a neuron adds up its weighted inputs, an activation function transforms that sum into the neuron's output. It decides how strongly, if at all, the neuron 'fires' or activates.

questions.question: A neuron generally combines its inputs by doing what before applying an activation?
options: Adding up the inputs multiplied by their weights
Deleting the smallest input
Sorting the inputs alphabetically
Saving the inputs to a file
questions.explanation: A neuron multiplies each input by its weight and sums the results to get a combined value.
questions.theory: A typical neuron multiplies each incoming value by its connection's weight and adds these products together. This weighted sum is then passed to an activation function to produce the output.

questions.question: Which everyday analogy best fits a single neuron?
options: A printer that copies pages
A calendar that tracks dates
A light switch that turns on when enough pressure is applied
A library that stores every book
questions.explanation: Like a switch that activates once a threshold is reached, a neuron produces a strong output when its inputs are large enough.
questions.theory: A neuron can be compared to a switch or dimmer: when its combined inputs are strong enough, it activates and passes a signal forward. This simple on/off-like behavior, repeated across many neurons, builds complex behavior.

questions.question: The hidden layers of a neural network are where most of the internal processing happens.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Hidden layers transform the input into useful internal representations before the output layer makes its decision.
questions.theory: Hidden layers sit between input and output and do much of the work of transforming raw data into useful patterns. The more capable hidden layers a network has, the more complex the patterns it can capture.

questions.question: When we say a neural network is 'trained,' what has mainly happened?
options: Its weights have been adjusted using data
Its screen has been cleaned
Its computer has been restarted
Its layers have been deleted
questions.explanation: Training means the network's weights have been tuned with example data so it makes better predictions.
questions.theory: Training is the process of showing a network many examples and adjusting its weights so its outputs improve. A trained network has weights that let it perform its task reasonably well.

questions.question: Why do we use the word 'network' to describe these systems?
options: Because many neurons are connected together
Because they use TV networks
Because they require the internet
Because they are made of fishing nets
questions.explanation: The term network reflects the many interconnected neurons that pass signals to one another.
questions.theory: A neural network is called a network because it consists of many neurons linked by connections. The pattern of these connections is what allows information to flow and be transformed.

questions.question: If a connection's weight is very close to zero, what does that usually mean?
options: The neuron will never produce output
That input is the most important
That input has little influence on the neuron
The network is broken
questions.explanation: A weight near zero means the connected input barely affects the receiving neuron's output.
questions.theory: The size of a weight reflects how much an input matters to a neuron. A weight near zero means that input has almost no effect, while a large weight means a strong effect.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of data you might feed into a neural network's input layer?
options: The number of hidden layers
The training time in seconds
The pixel values of a photo
The network's final prediction
questions.explanation: Input data is the raw information you give the network, such as the pixels of an image.
questions.theory: The input layer receives the raw features of an example, like the pixels of an image or the words of a sentence converted to numbers. This is the starting point for the network's computation.

questions.question: What happens to a neuron's output after it is produced (in a hidden layer)?
options: It replaces the input data
It is permanently deleted
It is printed on paper
It is passed along connections to neurons in the next layer
questions.explanation: A hidden neuron's output travels along its outgoing connections to feed neurons in the following layer.
questions.theory: Once a hidden neuron computes its output, that value is sent through its connections to neurons in the next layer. This chaining of outputs to inputs is how the network builds up a result.

questions.question: A network with one or more hidden layers can best be described as what?
options: Capable of learning more complex patterns than one with none
Always slower than the human brain
Unable to make predictions
The same as a single neuron
questions.explanation: Adding hidden layers lets a network capture more complex relationships in the data.
questions.theory: Hidden layers give a network the ability to combine inputs in richer ways, letting it model more complicated patterns. Networks with several hidden layers are often called deep neural networks.

questions.question: Every neuron in a neural network stores its own copy of the entire training dataset.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Neurons store weights, not the dataset; the data is used during training to adjust those weights.
questions.theory: A neuron holds simple numbers like weights, not the data itself. During training the data is used to update weights, but the finished network does not keep the dataset inside its neurons.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of giving a neural network many training examples?
options: So it can avoid using any layers
So it can adjust its weights to make better predictions
So the input layer can be removed
So it can fill up the hard drive
questions.explanation: Many examples let the network gradually tune its weights toward accurate predictions.
questions.theory: A network learns from examples by repeatedly checking its predictions and adjusting weights to reduce mistakes. The more good examples it sees, the better it can generalize to new cases.

questions.question: Which statement about activation is most accurate for a beginner?
options: Activation means turning off the computer
Activation is the name of the input file
Activation describes how strongly a neuron responds to its input
Activation is the color of the neuron
questions.explanation: Activation refers to the level of a neuron's response or output given its inputs.
questions.theory: A neuron's activation is its output value, reflecting how strongly it responds to the inputs it receives. An activation function decides this response from the neuron's weighted sum.

questions.question: In a simple network that recognizes handwritten digits, what would the output layer most likely represent?
options: The brand of the keyboard
The size of the screen
Which digit the image shows
The number of hidden layers
questions.explanation: For digit recognition, the output layer indicates which digit (0-9) the network thinks the image is.
questions.theory: The output layer is shaped to fit the task. For recognizing digits, it typically has outputs corresponding to each possible digit, and the strongest one is the network's guess.

questions.question: What does it mean for a neuron to have many incoming connections?
options: It can only work alone
It is part of the output only
It has no weights
It receives inputs from many other neurons
questions.explanation: Many incoming connections mean the neuron gathers and combines inputs from several other neurons.
questions.theory: A neuron can receive signals from many neurons in the previous layer, each through its own weighted connection. The neuron combines all of these inputs to compute its output.

questions.question: Which of these best describes 'learning' in a neural network?
options: Memorizing the user's name
Adding more screens to the computer
Turning the network off and on
Gradually changing weights to reduce mistakes
questions.explanation: Learning is the gradual adjustment of weights so the network's errors shrink over time.
questions.theory: Learning means the network improves at its task by changing its weights based on the errors it makes. Over many rounds, these small adjustments lead to better and better performance.

questions.question: What is a good simple analogy for how a neural network learns from feedback?
options: A clock that always shows the same time
A student adjusting their answers after seeing corrections
A rock sitting still on the ground
A locked box that never opens
questions.explanation: Like a student who improves after corrections, a network adjusts its weights after seeing its errors.
questions.theory: A network can be compared to a learner who practices, sees what they got wrong, and adjusts. Each correction nudges the weights so future answers are closer to correct.

questions.question: How many neurons does a neural network typically have?
options: Always exactly ten
Zero
Exactly one
Many, often arranged in layers
questions.explanation: Useful neural networks usually contain many neurons organized into layers.
questions.theory: Most neural networks contain many neurons grouped into layers. Working together, these many units let the network represent patterns that a single neuron could not.

questions.question: An activation function helps a neural network handle patterns that are not just straight-line relationships.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Activation functions add non-linearity, letting networks model curved and complex patterns.
questions.theory: Without activation functions, a network could only represent simple straight-line relationships. Activation functions introduce non-linearity, which lets the network capture more complex, curved patterns.

questions.question: What is the difference between the input layer and a hidden layer?
options: A hidden layer never has connections
The input layer holds the given data; a hidden layer processes it internally
There is no difference at all
The input layer is always the last layer
questions.explanation: The input layer simply holds the data you provide, while hidden layers transform it inside the network.
questions.theory: The input layer receives the raw data, whereas hidden layers take that data and transform it through weighted connections and activations. Hidden layers create the internal representations the output layer relies on.

questions.question: If you increase a weight on a connection, what generally happens to that input's effect on the neuron?
options: The neuron is deleted
Its effect on the neuron increases
Its effect disappears completely
The layer order reverses
questions.explanation: A larger weight makes the connected input have a stronger effect on the neuron's output.
questions.theory: Weights scale the influence of inputs. Increasing a weight makes the corresponding input count for more in the neuron's weighted sum, and thus more in its output.

questions.question: Which task is a neural network well suited for?
options: Charging a phone battery
Recognizing objects in photos
Cutting paper with scissors
Measuring room temperature with a thermometer
questions.explanation: Neural networks excel at pattern-recognition tasks like identifying objects in images.
questions.theory: Neural networks are particularly good at finding patterns in data, such as recognizing images, understanding speech, or predicting trends. They learn these patterns from examples rather than fixed rules.

questions.question: What do all the neurons in one layer typically do?
options: Stay completely inactive
Store the network's source code
Share a single output between them
Each computes its own output from the previous layer's values
questions.explanation: Each neuron in a layer independently computes its own output from the inputs it receives.
questions.theory: Within a layer, each neuron has its own weights and computes its own output from the previous layer's values. Together, the layer produces a set of outputs passed on to the next layer.

questions.question: Why are weights important to a neural network?
options: They decide the color of the screen
They are unrelated to learning
They count the number of users
They store what the network has learned
questions.explanation: Weights hold the knowledge a network gains during training, shaping how it responds to inputs.
questions.theory: The weights are where a network's learned knowledge lives. By tuning them during training, the network encodes the patterns it has discovered, which then determine its predictions.

questions.question: A 'deep' neural network is one that has what?
options: Many hidden layers
Only one neuron
No input layer
No weights at all
questions.explanation: Depth refers to having many hidden layers stacked between input and output.
questions.theory: The word deep in deep learning refers to networks with many hidden layers. More layers can let a network learn increasingly abstract and complex features from data.

questions.question: Before training begins, a network's weights are usually set how?
options: To exactly zero forever
To the correct final answers
To small random values
To the user's phone number
questions.explanation: Weights typically start as small random numbers and are then adjusted through training.
questions.theory: A network usually begins with random starting weights, so at first its predictions are poor. Training then gradually adjusts these weights until the network performs well.

questions.question: What is the relationship between a neuron's inputs and its output?
options: The output is computed from the inputs
Inputs and outputs are always equal
Inputs are computed from the output
The output is unrelated to the inputs
questions.explanation: A neuron's output is calculated from its inputs using weights and an activation function.
questions.theory: A neuron takes its inputs, combines them using weights, and applies an activation function to produce an output. So the output is a direct result of the inputs and the neuron's weights.

questions.question: Which of these is the best beginner description of a neural network?
options: A box that only stores files
A single fixed math formula that never changes
A system of connected units that learns patterns from data
A type of computer monitor
questions.explanation: A neural network is a collection of connected units that learns patterns by adjusting its weights.
questions.theory: At its core, a neural network is many connected neurons that learn patterns from data by tuning their weights. This lets it perform tasks like recognition and prediction without being explicitly programmed with rules.

questions.question: What flows along the connections between neurons?
options: Sound waves only
Paper documents
Electricity from a wall outlet
Numerical signals (values)
questions.explanation: Connections carry numerical values, which are scaled by their weights as they pass between neurons.
questions.theory: In an artificial neural network, the signals passing between neurons are numbers. Each connection scales the number it carries by its weight before delivering it to the next neuron.

questions.question: How does adding more neurons or layers generally affect a network?
options: It removes the input layer
It guarantees zero mistakes
It always makes the network forget everything
It can let the network learn more complex patterns
questions.explanation: More neurons and layers increase a network's capacity to represent complex patterns.
questions.theory: Adding neurons and layers increases a network's capacity, allowing it to capture more complex relationships. However, bigger networks also need more data and care to train well.

questions.question: What does it mean when we say a neuron 'fires' or 'activates'?
options: It disconnects from the network
It produces a strong output signal
It deletes itself
It catches on fire
questions.explanation: A neuron firing or activating means it produces a notable output passed to other neurons.
questions.theory: Borrowing from biology, we say a neuron fires or activates when it produces a strong output. The activation function decides how strongly the neuron responds to its combined input.

questions.question: Which part of the network do you directly provide data to?
options: The input layer
The activation function
A hidden layer
The output layer
questions.explanation: You supply your data to the input layer, which then passes it forward through the network.
questions.theory: The input layer is the entry point where you feed data into the network. From there, the data moves through hidden layers and finally to the output layer.

questions.question: Which part of the network do you read the answer from?
options: The input layer
The weights
The output layer
The first hidden layer
questions.explanation: The network's prediction is read from the output layer after the data has passed through.
questions.theory: The output layer is where the network's final answer appears. After data flows through all the layers, you interpret the output layer's values as the prediction.

questions.question: If a network keeps making the same mistake during training, what is typically adjusted to fix it?
options: Its screen brightness
The room temperature
The keyboard layout
Its weights
questions.explanation: Training fixes mistakes by adjusting the network's weights to reduce its errors.
questions.theory: When a network makes mistakes, the training process changes its weights to make the errors smaller next time. Repeating this over many examples is how the network improves.

questions.question: Which statement about layers is correct?
options: A network cannot have an output layer
A network has one input layer, one output layer, and zero or more hidden layers
A network must have exactly five hidden layers
Hidden layers come before the input layer
questions.explanation: A network always has an input and output layer, with any number of hidden layers in between.
questions.theory: Every neural network has an input layer and an output layer, and may have any number of hidden layers between them. The number of hidden layers depends on the problem and design choices.

questions.question: What is the best way to think about the knowledge a trained network holds?
options: It is kept on the keyboard
It is stored as a single photo
It is written as a paragraph of text
It is captured in the values of its weights
questions.explanation: A trained network's knowledge lives in the specific values of its weights.
questions.theory: When a network learns, the result is a set of weight values that capture the patterns it discovered. These weights, taken together, are the network's learned knowledge.

questions.question: How is an artificial neuron different from a real brain neuron?
options: It cannot process any inputs
It is exactly identical in every way
The artificial one is a simplified mathematical version
It is a living cell
questions.explanation: An artificial neuron is a simplified math model inspired by, but far simpler than, a biological neuron.
questions.theory: Artificial neurons are loosely inspired by biological ones but are much simpler mathematical units. They capture the basic idea of combining inputs and producing an output, without the full complexity of living cells.

questions.question: Why might a neural network give a wrong answer right after it is first created?
options: It has too many screens
Its weights start out random and untrained
Its output layer is missing
It was unplugged
questions.explanation: Before training, random starting weights mean the network has not yet learned to give good answers.
questions.theory: A freshly created network has random weights and has not learned anything, so its early answers are usually wrong. Training is what gradually turns those random weights into useful ones.

questions.question: What is the simplest summary of how a neural network makes a prediction?
options: It passes input through weighted connections and activations to produce an output
It looks up the answer in a dictionary
It asks a human for the answer
It generates the answer at random every time
questions.explanation: A network computes its prediction by sending input through weighted connections and activation functions to the output.
questions.theory: To make a prediction, a network takes the input, multiplies it by weights, applies activation functions through the layers, and reads the result at the output layer. This forward computation turns input data into an answer.

questions.question: Which combination correctly lists the three kinds of layers in a typical neural network?
options: Top, middle, and bottom drives
Input, hidden, and output layers
Red, green, and blue layers
Past, present, and future layers
questions.explanation: The three standard layer types are the input layer, hidden layer(s), and output layer.
questions.theory: A typical neural network is organized into an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer. Data enters at the input, is transformed in the hidden layers, and leaves at the output.

questions.question: In the forward pass of a feedforward network, what is computed at a single neuron before the activation function is applied?
options: A random sample from the inputs
The maximum of its inputs
A weighted sum of its inputs plus a bias
The gradient of the loss
questions.explanation: Each neuron forms a linear pre-activation: the dot product of weights with inputs plus a bias term.
questions.theory: A neuron's computation has two stages: first a linear combination z = w·x + b, then a nonlinear activation a = f(z). The forward pass propagates these activations layer by layer to produce the output.

questions.question: Why is a nonlinear activation function essential between layers of a deep network?
options: It removes the need for a bias term
It guarantees the loss is convex
Without it, stacked layers collapse into a single linear transformation
It makes the weights non-negative
questions.explanation: Composing linear layers yields another linear function, so nonlinearity is what lets depth represent complex mappings.
questions.theory: A composition of linear maps is itself linear. Inserting a nonlinearity between layers breaks this collapse, allowing the network to approximate nonlinear functions and form hierarchical features.

questions.question: What does the ReLU activation function output for an input value of -3?
questions.explanation: ReLU outputs max(0, x), so any negative input maps to 0.
questions.theory: ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) is defined as f(x) = max(0, x). It passes positive values unchanged and clamps negatives to zero, which is cheap to compute and helps mitigate vanishing gradients.

questions.question: The sigmoid activation function squashes its input into which output range?
options: (0, infinity)
(-infinity, infinity)
questions.explanation: Sigmoid maps any real number to a value strictly between 0 and 1.
questions.theory: The sigmoid function sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^-x) is S-shaped and outputs values in (0,1), making it useful for representing probabilities, though it can saturate and cause vanishing gradients for large magnitude inputs.

questions.question: What is the output range of the tanh activation function?
options: (0, infinity)
[0, infinity)
questions.explanation: Tanh outputs values between -1 and 1, making it zero-centered.
questions.theory: The hyperbolic tangent maps inputs to (-1, 1) and is zero-centered, which often helps optimization compared to sigmoid, though like sigmoid it saturates for large inputs.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of backpropagation in training a neural network?
options: To efficiently compute gradients of the loss with respect to every weight
To select the number of hidden layers
To initialize the weights randomly
To normalize the input features
questions.explanation: Backpropagation applies the chain rule to compute how each weight affects the loss.
questions.theory: Backpropagation is an algorithm that computes gradients of the loss with respect to all parameters by propagating error signals backward through the network using the chain rule, enabling gradient-based weight updates.

questions.question: Backpropagation relies on the chain rule of calculus to propagate error gradients from the output layer back toward the input layer.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: The chain rule lets gradients be assembled layer by layer from output to input.
questions.theory: Backpropagation is essentially repeated application of the chain rule. The gradient at each layer is the product of local derivatives and the gradient flowing back from later layers.

questions.question: In gradient descent, how is a weight updated once its gradient is known?
options: The weight is reset to zero
The weight moves in the direction opposite the gradient, scaled by the learning rate
The weight is multiplied by the gradient
The weight is set equal to the gradient
questions.explanation: Subtracting the learning-rate-scaled gradient moves the weight to reduce the loss.
questions.theory: Gradient descent updates each parameter as w := w - eta * dL/dw, where eta is the learning rate. Moving opposite the gradient decreases the loss, and the learning rate controls step size.

questions.question: What role does the loss function play during training?
options: It sets the number of neurons per layer
It randomizes the training data order
It chooses the activation function
It measures how far the network's predictions are from the targets
questions.explanation: The loss quantifies prediction error, providing the objective the network minimizes.
questions.theory: A loss function maps predictions and true targets to a single scalar measuring error. Training minimizes this scalar; its gradient with respect to the weights drives every update.

questions.question: What is the main role of hidden layers in a neural network?
options: To compute the final loss value
To store the training labels
To set the learning rate
To learn intermediate representations of the input
questions.explanation: Hidden layers transform inputs into increasingly useful intermediate features.
questions.theory: Hidden layers sit between input and output and learn intermediate feature representations. Stacking them lets the network build progressively more abstract transformations of the raw input.

questions.question: In the context of neural networks, what does the term "deep" most directly refer to?
options: Using very large input images
Having many layers between input and output
Using a high learning rate
Training for a very long time
questions.explanation: Depth refers to the number of stacked layers, not data size or training time.
questions.theory: "Deep" learning refers to networks with many hidden layers. Greater depth allows hierarchical feature learning, where each layer composes features from the previous one into more abstract representations.

questions.question: A network using only linear activations everywhere is equivalent to what kind of model regardless of its depth?
options: A decision tree
A convolutional network
A recurrent network
A single linear model
questions.explanation: Stacked linear layers compose into one linear transformation.
questions.theory: Because the composition of linear functions is linear, a purely linear deep network can represent only linear mappings, collapsing to an equivalent single-layer linear model no matter how many layers it has.

questions.question: Which problem is commonly associated with the sigmoid activation when inputs have large magnitude?
options: Exploding outputs above 1
Non-deterministic outputs
Vanishing gradients due to saturation
Negative probabilities
questions.explanation: In saturated regions the sigmoid's derivative approaches zero, shrinking gradients.
questions.theory: For large positive or negative inputs, sigmoid flattens and its gradient nears zero. During backpropagation these tiny gradients multiply across layers, causing the vanishing gradient problem that stalls learning in deep networks.

questions.question: What does the bias term in a neuron allow the activation to do?
options: Guarantee the output is positive
Shift the activation function left or right independent of the inputs
Make the gradient zero
Reduce the number of weights needed
questions.explanation: The bias lets a neuron activate at an input threshold other than zero.
questions.theory: The bias is an extra learnable parameter added to the weighted sum. It shifts the pre-activation, letting the neuron fire even when all inputs are zero and increasing the network's flexibility.

questions.question: During a single forward pass, in what order are layers computed?
options: In random order each pass
From output layer to input layer
All layers simultaneously and independently
From input layer toward the output layer
questions.explanation: Each layer needs the previous layer's activations, so computation flows input to output.
questions.theory: The forward pass evaluates layers sequentially from input to output because each layer consumes the activations produced by the layer before it, ultimately producing the network's prediction.

questions.question: Why can ReLU help reduce the vanishing gradient problem compared to sigmoid?
options: It outputs values in (0,1)
It scales inputs to unit variance
Its gradient is 1 for positive inputs rather than near zero
It is always differentiable everywhere
questions.explanation: For positive inputs ReLU's derivative is exactly 1, so gradients pass through undiminished.
questions.theory: ReLU has a constant gradient of 1 in its active region, so error signals do not shrink as they pass backward through positive units, helping gradients survive across many layers unlike saturating sigmoids.

questions.question: A neural network with no hidden layers and a linear output can only represent linear relationships between inputs and outputs.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: With no hidden layers and linear output, the model is just a linear function of inputs.
questions.theory: Hidden layers combined with nonlinear activations are what give networks the ability to model nonlinear relationships. Without them, the mapping from input to output remains strictly linear.

questions.question: What is an epoch in neural network training?
options: One complete pass through the entire training dataset
One weight update
The final trained model
One forward pass on a single example
questions.explanation: An epoch is one full sweep over all training examples.
questions.theory: An epoch denotes one complete presentation of the whole training set to the network. Training usually involves many epochs, with weights updated repeatedly as data is revisited.

questions.question: What does the learning rate control during gradient descent?
options: The batch ordering
The number of layers
The choice of loss function
The size of each step taken when updating weights
questions.explanation: The learning rate scales the gradient to set how far weights move each update.
questions.theory: The learning rate is a hyperparameter multiplying the gradient in the update rule. Too large a value can overshoot or diverge, while too small a value makes training slow.

questions.question: In a fully connected layer, how is each output neuron connected to the previous layer?
options: Only to neurons with positive activations
To exactly one neuron in the previous layer
To every neuron in the previous layer
To no neurons in the previous layer
questions.explanation: A fully connected (dense) layer links each output to all previous-layer activations.
questions.theory: In a dense layer, every neuron receives a weighted contribution from all neurons in the preceding layer. The layer is parameterized by a weight matrix whose entries are these connection strengths.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the softmax function in a classification network's output layer?
options: To compute the gradient of the loss
To convert raw scores into a probability distribution over classes
To normalize the input pixels
To randomly drop neurons
questions.explanation: Softmax turns logits into non-negative values that sum to one across classes.
questions.theory: Softmax exponentiates each output score and normalizes by the sum, producing a probability distribution over mutually exclusive classes. It is typically paired with cross-entropy loss for multi-class classification.

questions.question: Why is tanh sometimes preferred over sigmoid as a hidden-layer activation?
options: It always outputs probabilities
It has no derivative
Its outputs are zero-centered, which can aid optimization
It cannot saturate
questions.explanation: Zero-centered outputs keep activations balanced around zero, often easing gradient flow.
questions.theory: Tanh outputs lie in (-1,1) and are centered at zero, unlike sigmoid's (0,1) range. Zero-centered activations can make gradient updates more balanced, though tanh still saturates at its extremes.

questions.question: What is a "dead ReLU" problem?
options: A neuron stuck outputting zero for all inputs and no longer learning
A neuron that fires on every input
A neuron with negative weights
A neuron whose output always exceeds 1
questions.explanation: If a ReLU only sees negative pre-activations, it outputs zero and its gradient stays zero.
questions.theory: A dead ReLU occurs when a unit's weights drive its pre-activation persistently negative, so it always outputs zero. Its gradient is then zero, so it stops updating, effectively becoming inactive.

questions.question: In backpropagation, what is computed first before gradients can be propagated backward?
options: The final weight values
The number of epochs
The test accuracy
A forward pass to obtain activations and the loss
questions.explanation: Gradients depend on stored activations and the loss from a forward pass.
questions.theory: Training alternates a forward pass that computes activations and the loss with a backward pass that uses those stored values to compute gradients. The forward pass must precede the backward pass each iteration.

questions.question: What does mini-batch gradient descent do?
options: Updates weights using the gradient from one example at a time only
Updates weights only once after all epochs
Updates weights using gradients averaged over a small subset of examples
Never updates the weights
questions.explanation: A mini-batch averages gradients over a small group of samples per update.
questions.theory: Mini-batch gradient descent computes the gradient over a small batch of examples before each update. It balances the noisy efficiency of single-sample updates with the stability of full-batch gradients.

questions.question: Why does increasing depth (more layers) potentially help a network?
options: It removes the need for activation functions
It lets the network learn hierarchical, increasingly abstract features
It eliminates the need for data
It guarantees zero training error
questions.explanation: Deeper networks can compose simple features into more abstract ones layer by layer.
questions.theory: Depth enables hierarchical representation learning: early layers capture simple patterns and later layers combine them into abstract concepts. This compositional structure can represent complex functions more efficiently than shallow networks.

questions.question: What does the gradient of the loss with respect to a weight tell us?
options: The number of training examples
The exact optimal value of that weight
How much the loss changes for a small change in that weight
The activation function to use
questions.explanation: A gradient is a local sensitivity: the loss's rate of change as the weight varies.
questions.theory: The partial derivative dL/dw measures how sensitive the loss is to a tiny change in that weight. Its sign and magnitude indicate which direction and how strongly to adjust the weight to reduce loss.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the universal approximation idea for neural networks?
options: A deep network can memorize any dataset exactly
Only networks with thousands of layers can learn anything
Linear networks can approximate any function
A network with a single sufficiently wide hidden layer and nonlinearity can approximate a wide class of continuous functions
questions.explanation: One wide nonlinear hidden layer can approximate broad classes of continuous functions.
questions.theory: The universal approximation theorem states that a feedforward network with one hidden layer of enough units and a suitable nonlinear activation can approximate any continuous function on a bounded domain to arbitrary accuracy, though not necessarily efficiently.

questions.question: What is the derivative of ReLU for a strictly positive input?
options: The input value itself
Undefined
questions.explanation: For x > 0, ReLU is the identity, so its slope is 1.
questions.theory: ReLU equals x for positive inputs, giving a derivative of 1 there, and equals 0 for negative inputs, giving a derivative of 0. This piecewise-constant gradient is what makes it computationally simple.

questions.question: In a classification task, why is cross-entropy often preferred over mean squared error as the loss?
options: It requires no labels
It ignores the network's outputs
It pairs naturally with probability outputs and provides stronger gradients for confident wrong predictions
It cannot be negative
questions.explanation: Cross-entropy gives larger gradients when the model is confidently wrong, speeding learning.
questions.theory: Cross-entropy loss measures the divergence between predicted class probabilities and the true distribution. Combined with softmax it yields well-behaved gradients, often training faster than squared error on classification.

questions.question: The weights of a neural network are typically initialized to small random values rather than all zeros.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: All-zero weights make neurons in a layer identical, so they never differentiate.
questions.theory: If all weights start at zero, every neuron in a layer computes the same output and receives the same gradient, so they update identically. Random initialization breaks this symmetry and lets neurons learn distinct features.

questions.question: What does "forward pass" produce that is later needed by the backward pass?
options: The final test set
Intermediate activations used to compute gradients
The dataset labels
A new learning rate
questions.explanation: Backpropagation reuses the activations computed and cached during the forward pass.
questions.theory: Many gradient terms depend on the activations from the forward pass, so frameworks cache these intermediate values. The backward pass then combines them with incoming gradients via the chain rule.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key reason ReLU became popular in deep networks?
options: It outputs probabilities directly
It guarantees convex loss surfaces
It removes the need for weights
It is computationally cheap and helps preserve gradients in deep stacks
questions.explanation: ReLU is a simple max operation and keeps gradients from vanishing for active units.
questions.theory: ReLU requires only a comparison to zero, making it fast, and its unit gradient in the active region helps error signals propagate through many layers, both contributing to its widespread adoption in deep learning.

questions.question: What happens to learning if the learning rate is set far too high?
options: Updates can overshoot minima and the loss may diverge
Training converges faster with no downside
The network ignores the gradient entirely
The weights stop changing
questions.explanation: Oversized steps can jump past minima, causing the loss to oscillate or blow up.
questions.theory: A too-large learning rate causes weight updates to overshoot good regions of the loss surface, leading to oscillation or divergence. Tuning this hyperparameter is critical for stable convergence.

questions.question: In a network, what is meant by a neuron's "activation"?
options: Its position in the layer
The output value after applying the activation function
The number of connections it has
Its weighted-sum input before any function is applied
questions.explanation: The activation is the post-nonlinearity output the neuron passes onward.
questions.theory: A neuron first computes a pre-activation (weighted sum plus bias), then applies a nonlinear function to it. The resulting value, called the activation, is what feeds into the next layer.

questions.question: Why does stacking nonlinear layers increase a network's representational power?
options: It removes the need for training
It forces the output to be linear
Each layer can warp the space in ways linear layers cannot, enabling complex decision boundaries
It reduces the number of parameters to one
questions.explanation: Repeated nonlinear transformations let the network bend feature space into intricate shapes.
questions.theory: Nonlinear layers each apply a nonlinear warp to their inputs. Composing many such warps lets the network carve highly nonlinear decision boundaries and represent functions a single linear layer cannot.

questions.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: Gradients point in random directions
Gradients shrink toward zero as they propagate back through many layers, stalling early-layer learning
Gradients are always exactly zero
Gradients become extremely large and unstable
questions.explanation: Repeated multiplication of small derivatives shrinks gradients in early layers toward zero.
questions.theory: When activation derivatives are small, backpropagation multiplies many such factors, making gradients in early layers vanishingly small. Those layers then learn very slowly, a major obstacle for training very deep networks with saturating activations.

questions.question: What is the output of a sigmoid function when its input is exactly 0?
questions.theory: The sigmoid sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^-x) crosses 0.5 at x=0, its point of steepest slope. It approaches 1 for large positive inputs and 0 for large negative inputs.

questions.question: During training, what is being adjusted to reduce the loss?
options: The number of output classes
The order of the layers
The input data values
The network's weights and biases
questions.explanation: Learning changes weights and biases; the data and architecture stay fixed.
questions.theory: Training optimizes the learnable parameters, the weights and biases, by following gradients of the loss. The network architecture and the training data themselves are not modified by this process.

questions.question: What does it mean that backpropagation computes gradients "efficiently"?
options: It avoids computing any derivatives
It only works on linear networks
It requires evaluating the network once per weight
It reuses intermediate results so cost scales similarly to a forward pass rather than recomputing each derivative separately
questions.explanation: By caching and reusing intermediate terms, backprop avoids redundant per-weight recomputation.
questions.theory: Backpropagation applies the chain rule in a structured backward sweep, reusing shared intermediate quantities. This makes computing all gradients cost on the same order as a forward pass, rather than scaling with the number of parameters.

questions.question: In a multi-class network ending in softmax, what must the output values satisfy?
options: They sum to 0
They are each greater than 1
They sum to 1 and are each non-negative
They are all equal
questions.explanation: Softmax outputs form a valid probability distribution: non-negative and summing to one.
questions.theory: Softmax produces a probability distribution across classes, so its outputs are non-negative and sum to exactly 1. The largest output corresponds to the model's most likely predicted class.

questions.question: What is one consequence of the sigmoid not being zero-centered?
options: Gradients for a layer's weights tend to share the same sign, which can slow convergence
It cannot represent probabilities
It has no maximum value
Its outputs can be negative
questions.explanation: Always-positive activations push weight gradients to share signs, hampering smooth updates.
questions.theory: Because sigmoid outputs are always positive, the gradients flowing into a layer's weights tend to be all positive or all negative together, causing inefficient zig-zagging updates. Zero-centered activations like tanh avoid this.

questions.question: Which best describes the relationship between a loss surface and gradient descent?
options: Gradient descent climbs uphill to maximize loss
Gradient descent ignores the loss surface entirely
Gradient descent flattens the loss surface
Gradient descent walks downhill on the loss surface toward lower-loss regions
questions.explanation: Following the negative gradient moves parameters downhill toward lower loss.
questions.theory: The loss as a function of the weights forms a high-dimensional surface. Gradient descent uses the negative gradient as a downhill direction, iteratively stepping toward lower-loss regions and ideally a good minimum.

questions.question: Adding more hidden layers to a network always reduces error on unseen test data.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: More layers can overfit or be harder to train, sometimes worsening test performance.
questions.theory: While depth can increase representational capacity, more layers do not guarantee better generalization. Excess capacity can overfit the training data, and very deep networks can be harder to optimize without additional techniques.

questions.question: What does the chain rule contribute to computing a deep network's gradients?
options: It sets the weights to their final values
It removes the need for a loss function
It lets the gradient of the loss with respect to early weights be expressed as a product of local derivatives along the path
It determines the batch size
questions.explanation: The chain rule multiplies per-layer local derivatives to reach any weight's gradient.
questions.theory: For a composition of functions, the chain rule expresses an overall derivative as a product of local derivatives. Backpropagation uses this to chain per-layer derivatives from the loss back to any weight in the network.

questions.question: What is the pre-activation (often called z) of a neuron?
options: The output after the activation function
The neuron's index in its layer
The weighted sum of inputs plus bias, before the activation function
The gradient of the neuron
questions.explanation: The pre-activation z = w·x + b is the linear value fed into the activation function.
questions.theory: Each neuron computes z = w·x + b as its pre-activation, then applies a nonlinearity f to get the activation a = f(z). Distinguishing z from a is important when deriving backpropagation equations.

questions.question: Why might a leaky variant of ReLU be used instead of standard ReLU?
options: To bound the output to (0,1)
To allow a small nonzero gradient for negative inputs, reducing dead neurons
To force all outputs to be positive
To make the function linear everywhere
questions.explanation: A small negative-side slope keeps gradients flowing, preventing units from dying.
questions.theory: Leaky ReLU outputs a small multiple of the input for negative values instead of zero, so its gradient is never exactly zero there. This mitigates the dead-ReLU problem where units stop learning.

questions.question: What distinguishes the input layer from hidden layers in a feedforward network?
options: The input layer holds the raw feature values and does not transform them with learned weights
The input layer has the most neurons by definition
The input layer computes the loss
The input layer applies the strongest nonlinearity
questions.explanation: The input layer simply presents the raw features; transformation begins at the hidden layers.
questions.theory: The input layer represents the raw feature vector fed into the network and performs no learned transformation. Weighted transformations and nonlinearities begin at the first hidden layer.

questions.question: In one training iteration, what is the correct sequence of operations?
options: Forward pass, compute loss, backward pass to get gradients, update weights
Backward pass, forward pass, update weights, compute loss
Update weights, backward pass, forward pass, compute loss
Compute loss, update weights, forward pass, backward pass
questions.explanation: You must predict, measure error, compute gradients, then update parameters.
questions.theory: A standard training step runs the forward pass to produce predictions, evaluates the loss, performs backpropagation to compute gradients, and finally updates the weights with those gradients. This cycle repeats over many iterations.

questions.question: What is the main intuition behind why hidden layers enable feature hierarchies?
options: Each layer discards the previous layer's output
Each layer learns to combine the previous layer's features into more complex ones
Each layer reduces the data to a single number
Each layer copies the input unchanged
questions.explanation: Successive layers build complex features by combining simpler ones from below.
questions.theory: Hidden layers transform representations sequentially, so deeper layers can compose simpler patterns detected earlier into higher-level, more abstract features. This layered composition is the core intuition behind hierarchical feature learning.

questions.question: In a deep feedforward network using sigmoid activations, why do gradients tend to vanish as they propagate backward through many layers?
options: Backpropagation only updates the final layer and ignores earlier ones
Weights are initialized to exactly zero, so no gradient can flow
The loss function becomes undefined when too many layers are stacked
The chain rule multiplies many sigmoid derivatives, each at most 0.25, shrinking the product toward zero
questions.explanation: Each sigmoid derivative is bounded by 0.25, so multiplying many of them during backpropagation drives the gradient exponentially toward zero.
questions.theory: The vanishing gradient problem arises because backpropagation multiplies local derivatives across layers via the chain rule. When those derivatives are consistently less than one, the resulting gradient for early layers becomes vanishingly small, stalling their learning.

questions.question: Which property of the ReLU activation most directly helps mitigate vanishing gradients compared to sigmoid?
options: It guarantees gradients can never explode
It has a derivative of exactly 1 for all positive inputs, preserving gradient magnitude
It squashes all inputs into the range (0,1)
It is differentiable everywhere including at zero
questions.explanation: For positive inputs ReLU has a constant derivative of 1, so gradients pass through unattenuated rather than being repeatedly shrunk.
questions.theory: ReLU outputs the input directly when positive and zero otherwise. Its unit slope in the positive region keeps gradient magnitudes from decaying across layers, which is a key reason deep networks train more easily with ReLU than with saturating activations.

questions.question: Exploding gradients are most commonly addressed in recurrent networks by which technique?
options: Using a larger batch size to average out the explosion
Gradient clipping, which rescales gradients whose norm exceeds a threshold
Removing the recurrent connections entirely
Increasing the learning rate to push past the instability
questions.explanation: Gradient clipping caps the gradient norm at a chosen threshold, preventing the destabilizing parameter updates that exploding gradients cause.
questions.theory: In deep or recurrent networks, repeated multiplication of large Jacobian terms can make gradients grow exponentially. Clipping rescales the gradient vector when its norm is too large, keeping updates bounded while preserving their direction.

questions.question: Residual (skip) connections help train very deep networks largely because they provide a path for gradients to flow backward without being repeatedly multiplied by intermediate weight matrices.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: The identity shortcut lets gradients bypass the transformation layers, so the signal reaching early layers is not attenuated by every intermediate factor.
questions.theory: Residual connections add a layer's input directly to its output. During backpropagation this creates an additive identity term in the gradient, giving an unimpeded route for the error signal and substantially reducing vanishing-gradient effects in very deep architectures.

questions.question: What is the primary architectural reason convolutional neural networks are well suited to image data?
options: They share weights across spatial locations, exploiting translation invariance and reducing parameters
They cannot represent any spatial hierarchy of features
They process pixels strictly one at a time in raster order
They require fully connected layers between every pair of pixels
questions.explanation: Weight sharing via convolutional filters detects the same local pattern anywhere in the image while drastically cutting parameter count.
questions.theory: CNNs apply small learnable filters that slide across the input, reusing the same weights at every position. This exploits the spatial structure and translation invariance of images, letting the network learn local-to-global feature hierarchies efficiently.

questions.question: Which statement best captures the high-level purpose for which recurrent neural networks were originally designed?
options: Rendering high-resolution images from noise
Performing matrix factorization on static tabular data
Detecting spatially local features in fixed-size images
Processing sequential data by maintaining a hidden state that carries information across time steps
questions.explanation: RNNs maintain a recurrent hidden state that is updated at each step, letting them model dependencies in ordered sequences.
questions.theory: Recurrent networks feed their hidden state forward through a sequence, so each output depends on prior inputs. This makes them naturally suited to time series, language, and other ordered data where context from earlier elements matters.

questions.question: What core mechanism distinguishes the transformer architecture from RNNs for sequence modeling?
options: A fixed lookup table with no learnable parameters
Self-attention, which lets every position directly attend to every other position in parallel
Convolutional filters that only see a fixed local window
A single recurrent cell applied sequentially across time
questions.explanation: Self-attention computes pairwise interactions between all positions simultaneously, removing the sequential bottleneck of recurrence.
questions.theory: Transformers replace recurrence with self-attention, where each token forms a weighted combination of all other tokens. Because these computations are parallel across the sequence, transformers train efficiently and capture long-range dependencies without unrolling through time.

questions.question: Why can transformers capture long-range dependencies more easily than vanilla RNNs?
options: They ignore positional information entirely, so distance is irrelevant
They use larger learning rates that reach distant tokens faster
They process sequences in reverse, which shortens dependencies
Attention connects distant positions with a constant path length rather than many sequential steps
questions.explanation: In self-attention any two positions interact directly, so the effective path length between them is constant rather than growing with their separation.
questions.theory: In an RNN, information between two distant tokens must pass through every intermediate step, where it can decay. Self-attention links any pair of positions in a single operation, giving a constant-length path that preserves long-range signal.

questions.question: In the intuition behind attention, what role do the query and key vectors play?
options: They are fixed constants that never change during training
Their similarity determines how much weight a position assigns to each other position's value
They store the final output of the network directly
They replace the loss function during backpropagation
questions.explanation: The dot product of a query with each key yields the attention scores that weight the corresponding values.
