questions.theory: Hallucination refers to confidently generated content that is not grounded in reality. Because the model optimizes for plausible token sequences, fabricated references can read convincingly even though they are false.

questions.question: How do messages with different roles (system, user, assistant) typically appear to the model in a chat request?
options: As three completely separate models running in parallel
As a structured sequence of role-labeled messages combined into the input the model processes
As random noise that the model ignores
As image inputs rather than text
questions.explanation: Role-labeled messages are assembled into a single structured input the model reads in order.
questions.theory: Chat-formatted requests tag each message with a role and order them into one sequence. The model conditions on this full structured history, using role labels to distinguish instructions, user input, and prior replies.

questions.question: Which statement about an LLM's knowledge is most accurate?
options: It continuously learns from the live internet during every conversation
It stores a complete copy of the internet that refreshes hourly
It has perfect, real-time knowledge of all current events
Its knowledge is bounded by its training data and does not automatically update after training
questions.explanation: An LLM's knowledge is frozen at training time and does not self-update afterward.
questions.theory: A model's parameters encode patterns from a fixed training corpus gathered up to some point. Without retraining or external tools, it cannot know events or facts that emerged after that data was collected.

questions.question: What is a key benefit of using embeddings for search compared to exact keyword matching?
options: Embeddings can retrieve conceptually related results even when the exact keywords differ
Embeddings make all documents equally relevant
Embeddings only match documents that contain the identical words
Embeddings eliminate the need to store any documents
questions.explanation: Embedding search captures meaning, so it finds related content even without shared keywords.
questions.theory: Keyword search relies on literal term overlap, whereas embedding-based search compares semantic vectors. This lets it surface relevant documents that express the same idea using different words, improving recall on paraphrased queries.

questions.question: Why might lowering temperature be preferred for tasks like data extraction or classification?
options: Lower temperature makes the model more creative and exploratory
Lower temperature increases the context window for extraction
Lower temperature is required to load the tokenizer
Lower temperature yields more consistent, predictable outputs suited to structured tasks
questions.explanation: Lower temperature produces stable, predictable output that benefits structured tasks.
questions.theory: Extraction and classification need reliable, repeatable answers rather than creative variation. Reducing temperature concentrates probability on the most likely tokens, making the model's responses more deterministic and consistent.

questions.question: Because LLMs predict tokens based on probability, they can produce grammatically fluent text that is factually incorrect.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Fluency reflects likely token sequences, which does not guarantee factual correctness.
questions.theory: Language models optimize for plausible continuations, and fluency is a property of the token statistics rather than truth. As a result, well-formed sentences can still contain inaccurate or fabricated claims.

questions.question: What does 'autoregressive generation' mean for how an LLM produces a response?
options: It generates one token at a time, feeding each produced token back in to predict the next
It writes the response backward from the last token to the first
It outputs the entire response simultaneously in a single parallel step with no ordering
It selects a complete sentence from a fixed list of stored answers
questions.explanation: Autoregressive models generate sequentially, conditioning each new token on the ones already produced.
questions.theory: In autoregressive decoding, the model predicts the next token, appends it, and repeats, so each step depends on all prior tokens. This sequential process is why generation length affects latency.

questions.question: Which approach best reduces hallucinations when answering factual questions?
options: Raising the temperature to encourage more answers
Grounding the model with retrieved, relevant source text it can reference in the prompt
Removing the system prompt entirely
Shrinking the vocabulary of the tokenizer
questions.explanation: Providing relevant source text grounds answers and reduces fabrication.
questions.theory: Retrieval-augmented approaches insert authoritative passages into the prompt so the model can base answers on them. Grounding the generation in real evidence substantially lowers the rate of fabricated facts.

questions.question: How does tokenization affect multilingual text, where some languages use scripts underrepresented in training?
options: Non-Latin scripts are never tokenizable
Such text always uses fewer tokens than English
Tokenization is identical across all languages and scripts
Such text may be split into more tokens, consuming more of the context budget per character
questions.explanation: Underrepresented scripts often fragment into more tokens, using more of the context budget.
questions.theory: Tokenizer vocabularies are shaped by training-data frequencies, so scripts that appear less often may lack compact tokens. Text in those languages can require more tokens per character, reducing effective context capacity.

questions.question: What is the practical meaning of 'grounding' an LLM's response?
options: Disabling the model's ability to generate text
Constraining or informing the output using provided, verifiable context rather than relying solely on parametric memory
Physically connecting the server to an electrical ground
Forcing the model to output only one token
questions.explanation: Grounding means basing the answer on supplied, verifiable context instead of internal memory alone.
questions.theory: Grounding ties a model's output to external evidence supplied in the prompt, such as documents or data. This makes responses more accurate and verifiable than relying purely on knowledge baked into the weights.

questions.question: Why is it said that an LLM does not truly 'understand' that it lacks knowledge of a topic?
options: It always returns an explicit error when it lacks knowledge
It refuses to generate any tokens for unknown topics
It consults a built-in confidence database before every answer
It predicts plausible tokens regardless of whether it has reliable information, so it may answer confidently anyway
questions.explanation: The model generates plausible tokens even without reliable knowledge, so confidence is not calibrated to truth.
questions.theory: An LLM has no inherent mechanism to assess whether it knows something; it simply produces likely continuations. This is why it can respond with unwarranted confidence on topics it has little reliable training signal for.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of placing few-shot examples in a prompt?
options: To switch the model into a different language entirely
To permanently fine-tune the model on those examples
To reduce the token cost to zero
To demonstrate the desired input-output pattern so the model imitates it for new inputs
questions.explanation: Few-shot examples show the model the pattern to follow without altering its weights.
questions.theory: Few-shot prompting provides a handful of solved examples that illustrate the expected format and behavior. The model generalizes from these in-context demonstrations to handle new, similar inputs without any retraining.

questions.question: Which statement about the context window and conversation cost is correct?
options: Only the very first message in a conversation is ever billed
Conversation length has no effect on per-request token counts
Resending history reduces the number of tokens processed
As a conversation grows and more history is resent, each request can include more tokens and cost more
questions.explanation: Resending accumulating history increases tokens per request, raising cost as conversations grow.
questions.theory: Since statelessness requires resending prior turns, longer conversations carry more history into each request. That growing token count typically increases both the cost and the latency of subsequent calls.

questions.question: What distinguishes a base (pretrained) model from an instruction-tuned model?
options: The base model is always larger and more capable at every task
The instruction-tuned model has been further trained to follow instructions and respond helpfully, while the base model mainly continues text
The instruction-tuned model has no weights
The base model can only output a single token
questions.explanation: Instruction tuning teaches a base model to follow directions, whereas a base model mostly continues text.
questions.theory: A base model is pretrained to predict next tokens and tends to continue or complete text. Instruction tuning adds further training on instruction-response pairs so the model better follows user directions and behaves as an assistant.

questions.question: Why can two visually similar strings, like a word with and without a trailing space, tokenize differently?
options: Trailing spaces double the size of the context window
Tokenizers are sensitive to exact characters, including whitespace, which can change how text is segmented
Whitespace is always stripped before tokenization, so they are identical
Tokenizers ignore the difference and produce the same tokens
questions.explanation: Whitespace is part of the input, so it can alter how a tokenizer segments text.
questions.theory: Subword tokenizers often treat leading or trailing whitespace as meaningful and may bundle it with adjacent characters. As a result, otherwise similar strings can map to different token sequences depending on spacing.

questions.question: What is a sound reason to prefer a higher temperature?
options: When extracting fields into a strict schema
When generating creative content like brainstorming, where diverse and varied outputs are desirable
When you need the single most precise, deterministic factual answer
When you want identical output every time
questions.explanation: Higher temperature suits creative tasks that benefit from varied, diverse output.
questions.theory: Raising temperature increases output diversity, which helps with open-ended generation such as brainstorming or storytelling. For tasks demanding precision and repeatability, lower temperatures are usually preferred.

questions.question: How should an application treat the assistant's previous reply in a multi-turn chat?
options: Include it as part of the conversation history sent in the next request so the model has context
Discard it immediately since the model already remembers it internally
Store it only as an embedding and never as text
Convert it into a system prompt that overrides all user input
questions.explanation: Prior assistant replies must be resent as history because the model retains no internal memory.
questions.theory: Given statelessness, the assistant's earlier responses are part of the context the application must resend. Including them lets the model maintain coherence and reference what it previously said.

questions.question: Which best explains why embeddings are described as 'dense' vectors?
options: They contain mostly zeros with a single nonzero entry
They have exactly two dimensions in all cases
Most or all of their dimensions hold meaningful nonzero values, unlike sparse one-hot representations
They are stored only as plain text strings
questions.explanation: Dense embeddings use many nonzero dimensions, in contrast to sparse representations dominated by zeros.
questions.theory: Dense embeddings pack semantic information across many continuous dimensions, each contributing to the representation. This contrasts with sparse one-hot vectors, which are mostly zero and carry no notion of similarity.

questions.question: What is a realistic limitation of relying on an LLM's internal knowledge for arithmetic with large numbers?
options: It refuses to output any digits
It converts numbers into images before answering
It may produce plausible-looking but incorrect results because it predicts tokens rather than computing exactly
It always computes large arithmetic perfectly with no errors
questions.explanation: The model predicts likely tokens rather than calculating, so large-number arithmetic can be wrong.
questions.theory: LLMs generate arithmetic answers from learned token patterns instead of performing exact computation. For large or complex calculations, this can yield confident yet incorrect results, which is why external tools are often used.

questions.question: Why does providing a clear, specific system prompt often improve output quality?
options: It constrains the model's behavior and reduces ambiguity about the desired role, tone, and constraints
It increases the model's parameter count during the call
It guarantees the model can access live external data
It removes the need to send any user prompt
questions.explanation: A clear system prompt reduces ambiguity, steering the model toward the intended behavior.
questions.theory: A well-specified system prompt sets expectations for role, tone, and limits, narrowing the space of likely outputs. Reducing ambiguity helps the model produce responses that better match the user's intent.

questions.question: What does it mean that an LLM processes its entire prompt as a sequence of tokens?
options: The model reads raw characters one letter at a time with no conversion
The model treats the prompt as a single indivisible token
The model only accepts inputs already in vector form from the user
Text is converted into token IDs before the model operates on it, and outputs are token IDs decoded back to text
questions.explanation: Prompts are encoded into token IDs for the model and its token outputs are decoded back to text.
questions.theory: Before inference, the tokenizer maps text to integer token IDs that the model can process. The model emits token IDs in turn, which are decoded back into human-readable text for the response.

questions.question: Which is the most accurate reason fine-tuning requires careful, representative data?
options: Fine-tuning ignores the data and only changes the temperature
Any random data fine-tunes the model equally well
Fine-tuning data only affects the tokenizer, not behavior
The model adjusts its weights toward the provided examples, so biased or low-quality data degrades behavior
questions.explanation: Since fine-tuning shifts weights toward the examples, poor or biased data harms model behavior.
questions.theory: Fine-tuning updates parameters to fit the supplied examples, so the model inherits patterns and biases present in that data. Representative, high-quality datasets are essential to avoid degrading or skewing the model's behavior.

questions.question: What is the best general description of how an LLM 'decides' what to say next?
options: It chooses tokens uniformly at random with no relation to the prompt
It asks a human reviewer for each token in real time
It looks up the answer in a fixed table indexed by the prompt
It computes a probability distribution over possible next tokens and samples or selects from it according to the decoding settings
questions.explanation: The model forms a next-token probability distribution and picks from it per the decoding settings.
questions.theory: At each step the model outputs a distribution over its vocabulary conditioned on the prior tokens. Decoding settings such as temperature and top-p then determine how a token is chosen from that distribution.

questions.question: In retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), what is the primary architectural reason it can reduce hallucination relative to a parametric-only model?
options: It fine-tunes the model weights on every query at inference time
It grounds generation on externally retrieved context supplied in the prompt, shifting reliance from memorized parameters to provided evidence
It replaces the transformer decoder with a symbolic logic engine
It increases the softmax temperature so outputs become more deterministic
questions.explanation: RAG conditions generation on retrieved passages placed in context, so the model can cite provided evidence rather than relying solely on parametric memory.
questions.theory: RAG augments a generator with a retriever that fetches relevant documents from an external corpus. The retrieved text is concatenated into the prompt, grounding the output and making knowledge updatable without retraining.

questions.question: A RAG pipeline retrieves passages by cosine similarity in a dense embedding space. Which failure mode is MOST directly caused by an embedding model that maps a query and a relevant-but-differently-worded passage far apart?
options: Prompt injection from the retrieved text
Tokenizer mismatch between encoder and decoder
Low recall: relevant documents are not retrieved despite being semantically on-topic
Excessive context window truncation of the system prompt
questions.explanation: If the embedding model fails to place semantically related query and passage vectors near each other, relevant documents are missed, lowering retrieval recall.
questions.theory: Dense retrieval depends on an embedding model placing semantically similar texts close in vector space. Poor representations cause low recall (missing relevant docs) or low precision (returning irrelevant ones), bounding the quality of the whole RAG system.

questions.question: In a standard RAG setup, expanding the retriever's corpus with more documents guarantees the generator's final answer accuracy will improve.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: More documents can add noise and distractors that degrade retrieval precision and confuse the generator, so accuracy is not guaranteed to improve.
questions.theory: RAG quality is bottlenecked by retrieval precision and the generator's ability to use context. Adding documents increases coverage but can introduce near-duplicate or distracting passages, sometimes lowering end-to-end accuracy.

questions.question: Why is a re-ranking stage (e.g., a cross-encoder) often added after an initial dense or sparse retrieval step in RAG?
options: To convert dense vectors into sparse keyword indexes
To jointly encode the query and each candidate passage together, producing a more accurate relevance score than independent embeddings
To compress the documents into fewer tokens before storage
To generate the final answer directly without the language model
questions.explanation: A cross-encoder re-ranker processes the query and candidate together with full attention, yielding finer-grained relevance than the bi-encoder used for fast first-stage retrieval.
questions.theory: Two-stage retrieval uses a fast bi-encoder to fetch many candidates, then a slower cross-encoder that attends over the query and passage jointly to re-rank the top results. This trades extra compute for higher precision on the documents actually fed to the generator.

questions.question: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) typically trains a separate reward model. What does that reward model learn to predict?
options: The retrieval relevance of external documents
A scalar score reflecting human preference between candidate model outputs
The exact gradient of the base model's loss
The next token in the pretraining corpus
questions.explanation: The reward model is trained on human preference comparisons to output a scalar that approximates how much humans favor a given response.
questions.theory: In RLHF, humans rank or compare model outputs; a reward model is fit to these preferences to produce a scalar reward. A policy-gradient method (commonly PPO) then optimizes the language model to maximize this learned reward while staying close to the reference policy.

questions.question: In RLHF with PPO, a KL-divergence penalty against the initial supervised (reference) policy is commonly included. What is its main purpose?
options: To replace the reward model entirely
To increase output diversity by maximizing entropy without bound
To prevent the policy from drifting too far from coherent language and exploiting reward-model weaknesses
To accelerate gradient descent by normalizing the learning rate
questions.explanation: The KL penalty keeps the optimized policy near the reference distribution, mitigating reward hacking and degeneration of fluent text.
questions.theory: Optimizing purely for a learned reward can push the policy into out-of-distribution regions where the reward model is inaccurate (reward hacking). A KL term anchored to the reference policy constrains updates, balancing reward maximization against staying linguistically coherent.

questions.question: 'Reward hacking' in alignment refers to which phenomenon?
options: The policy achieving high reward-model scores via behaviors that don't reflect the intended human preference
The reward model being stolen by an external attacker
Human labelers intentionally mislabeling preference data
Gradient explosion during reward model training
questions.explanation: Reward hacking occurs when the model exploits imperfections in the proxy reward to score highly without genuinely satisfying the underlying objective.
questions.theory: A learned reward model is an imperfect proxy for true human values. Optimization pressure can surface inputs where the proxy and the true objective diverge, so the policy maximizes the measurable signal (e.g., verbosity or flattery) rather than the intended goal.

questions.question: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) differs from classic PPO-based RLHF primarily in that it:
options: Uses retrieval instead of human feedback
Requires no preference data at all
Eliminates the need for a pretrained base model
Optimizes a classification-style loss directly on preference pairs without training an explicit separate reward model or running online RL
questions.explanation: DPO reparameterizes the preference objective so the policy is updated directly from preference pairs, avoiding a standalone reward model and the online RL loop.
questions.theory: DPO derives a loss equivalent to the RLHF objective but expressible directly in terms of the policy and a reference model. It treats alignment as a supervised-style binary preference classification, simplifying the pipeline compared to PPO while pursuing the same goal.

questions.question: In-context learning lets an LLM adapt to a task from examples in the prompt. What is the key distinction from fine-tuning?
options: Fine-tuning works only at inference time, in-context learning only at training
In-context learning permanently updates model weights; fine-tuning does not
In-context learning conditions behavior via the prompt at inference with no weight update; fine-tuning changes parameters
They are identical mechanisms with different names
questions.explanation: In-context learning adapts behavior purely through the conditioning context at inference, whereas fine-tuning modifies the model's weights.
questions.theory: During in-context learning, demonstrations in the prompt steer the model's conditional distribution without any gradient updates; the learned weights are fixed. Fine-tuning instead performs gradient descent that alters parameters, persisting across all future inferences.

questions.question: Empirically, how does the ORDER of few-shot examples in a prompt typically affect classification performance?
options: It has no measurable effect because attention is permutation-invariant
It only matters when examples exceed the context window
It can substantially change accuracy, revealing sensitivity and potential ordering bias
It affects only the tokenizer, not the model output
questions.explanation: Few-shot accuracy is known to be sensitive to example ordering, so permutations can meaningfully shift predictions.
questions.theory: Transformer attention is not permutation-invariant over positions because of positional encodings, and autoregressive models condition on earlier tokens. Consequently, the arrangement and selection of demonstrations can introduce recency or majority-label biases that affect outcomes.

questions.question: Chain-of-thought prompting improves performance on multi-step reasoning largely because it:
options: Disables the attention mechanism to reduce interference
Allocates intermediate generated tokens as a scratchpad, letting the model decompose the problem before committing to an answer
Increases the model's parameter count during inference
Retrieves the answer from an external database
questions.explanation: Generating intermediate reasoning tokens gives the model additional serial computation and working space to decompose multi-step problems.
questions.theory: Each generated token is produced by a fixed-depth forward pass, so a single token cannot perform arbitrarily long serial reasoning. Chain-of-thought externalizes intermediate steps as tokens, effectively extending computation across the sequence and improving multi-step accuracy.

questions.question: At a high level, self-attention computes each output position as a weighted sum of value vectors. What determines those weights?
options: The gradient of the loss with respect to the embeddings
Scaled dot-product similarities between the position's query vector and all key vectors, passed through a softmax
A random sampling temperature applied per head
The fixed positional indices alone, independent of content
questions.explanation: Attention weights come from softmax over scaled query-key dot products, so they are content-dependent similarity scores.
questions.theory: In scaled dot-product attention, each token emits query, key, and value vectors. The compatibility of a query with each key (scaled by the square root of the head dimension) is softmax-normalized into weights that mix the value vectors, letting tokens dynamically attend to relevant context.

questions.question: Why does standard self-attention have computational and memory cost that scales quadratically with sequence length?
options: Because each layer doubles the embedding dimension
Because positional encodings are recomputed for each parameter
Because every token attends to every other token, forming an n-by-n attention matrix
Because the feed-forward network is applied n-squared times
questions.explanation: Full attention compares all pairs of tokens, producing an n-by-n score matrix whose size grows with the square of sequence length.
questions.theory: Dense self-attention computes pairwise interactions among all n tokens, so the attention score matrix and its softmax require O(n squared) time and memory. This motivates long-context techniques such as sparse, low-rank, or memory-efficient attention to reduce the scaling.

questions.question: In a decoder-only transformer language model, the causal (autoregressive) attention mask ensures that:
options: All positions are masked equally to enforce uniform attention
Each position can attend to future positions to plan ahead
Padding tokens receive the highest attention weights
Each position attends only to itself and earlier positions, preserving left-to-right generation
questions.explanation: The causal mask blocks attention to future tokens so predictions depend only on preceding context, matching autoregressive generation.
questions.theory: Autoregressive language models factorize the joint probability as a product of next-token conditionals. A causal mask zeroes attention to positions to the right, guaranteeing that each token's representation is a function of itself and prior tokens only, consistent with sequential generation.

questions.question: Multi-head attention exists primarily so the model can attend to information from different representation subspaces and positions simultaneously.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Multiple heads project into distinct subspaces, allowing the model to capture several relationship types in parallel.
questions.theory: Multi-head attention runs several attention operations in parallel on separately projected query, key, and value subspaces. Each head can specialize in different relational patterns, and their concatenated outputs give the model richer, more expressive context mixing than a single head.

questions.question: During autoregressive decoding, a key-value (KV) cache is used to:
options: Reuse previously computed keys and values for past tokens so each new token avoids recomputing them
Retrieve external documents for RAG
Permanently store the training dataset in memory
Compress the model weights to half precision
questions.explanation: The KV cache stores keys and values from prior positions so generating each new token only computes attention against cached states rather than reprocessing the whole prefix.
questions.theory: In autoregressive generation, the keys and values of earlier tokens do not change as new tokens are produced. Caching them lets the model compute attention for the new query against stored states, turning per-step cost from quadratic-in-prefix to roughly linear, at the price of memory proportional to context length.

questions.question: Scaling-law studies suggest that for a fixed compute budget, the most loss-efficient strategy generally involves:
options: Training a tiny model on an essentially unlimited number of tokens
Maximizing parameters while keeping training tokens minimal
Holding both data and model size fixed and only raising the learning rate
Jointly increasing model size AND training data, since both must scale together for compute-optimal training
questions.explanation: Compute-optimal scaling balances growth in parameters and training tokens together rather than scaling only one.
questions.theory: Empirical scaling laws relate test loss to model size, dataset size, and compute via smooth power laws. Compute-optimal results indicate that for a given budget, parameters and data should be scaled in tandem; an oversized model trained on too little data wastes compute relative to a balanced allocation.

questions.question: Neural scaling laws are typically observed to follow which functional form relating test loss to scale (parameters, data, or compute)?
options: Exponential decay to zero loss
Smooth power-law decline with diminishing returns, approaching an irreducible loss floor
Linear decrease without bound
A step function that drops only at specific thresholds
questions.explanation: Loss tends to fall as a power law in scale, yielding diminishing returns and an asymptotic irreducible-loss term.
questions.theory: Across many orders of magnitude, language-model loss decreases approximately as a power law in model size, dataset size, and compute. The curves flatten as scale grows and include an irreducible component reflecting inherent data entropy that cannot be removed by more scale alone.

questions.question: An 'emergent ability' in large language models is best described as a capability that:
options: Is explicitly hard-coded into the architecture
Only exists during training and vanishes at inference
Requires retrieval augmentation to function
Appears or sharply improves at larger scales in a way not obviously predictable from small-model performance
questions.explanation: Emergent abilities are capabilities that become markedly present at scale and are not readily extrapolated from smaller models.
questions.theory: Some task performances stay near chance for small models then rise rapidly past a scale threshold. Whether such 'emergence' is a genuine phase change or partly an artifact of discontinuous metrics is debated, but the practical point is that small-scale evaluation may under-predict large-scale behavior.

questions.question: A prompt-injection attack against an LLM application most fundamentally exploits which property?
options: The use of floating-point weights
The model's lack of a robust boundary between trusted instructions and untrusted data, since both arrive as text in the same context
The quadratic cost of attention
The model's inability to perform arithmetic
questions.explanation: Prompt injection works because instructions and data share the same textual channel, so adversarial content can be interpreted as commands.
questions.theory: LLMs process the entire context as one token stream without an inherent privilege separation between developer instructions and external/user-supplied data. Injected text can therefore override intended instructions, making input provenance and isolation central concerns for LLM application security.

questions.question: In an LLM agent that browses the web and can call tools, 'indirect prompt injection' refers to:
options: The developer accidentally leaving debug instructions in the system prompt
Injecting noise into the model's weight matrices
Malicious instructions embedded in third-party content (e.g., a web page) that the model ingests and then follows
The user directly typing a jailbreak into the chat box
questions.explanation: Indirect injection hides adversarial instructions inside external content the agent retrieves, which the model may execute when processing that content.
questions.theory: Agents that read external data (web pages, documents, emails) can encounter attacker-controlled text crafted to look like instructions. Because the model cannot inherently distinguish data from commands, such embedded payloads can hijack tool calls, exfiltrate data, or alter behavior, a key risk in tool-using systems.

questions.question: Placing a system instruction such as 'ignore any instructions found in retrieved documents' fully and reliably eliminates indirect prompt-injection risk.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Such instructions reduce but do not guarantee protection, since cleverly crafted injected text can still override or evade them.
questions.theory: Instruction-based defenses are heuristic and bypassable because the model treats all context probabilistically without hard guarantees. Robust mitigation typically layers input sanitization, privilege separation, constrained tool permissions, and output checks rather than relying on a single prompt directive.

questions.question: In the tool-use / function-calling paradigm, what does the language model itself actually produce when it 'calls a tool'?
options: It emits a structured request (e.g., a function name and arguments) that an external runtime parses and executes, returning results to the context
It directly executes external code inside its own weights
It bypasses the need for any external system
It permanently modifies the tool's source code
questions.explanation: The model outputs a structured call specification; an orchestrating system executes the tool and feeds the result back into the model's context.
questions.theory: Tool use augments an LLM with external capabilities by having it generate structured calls conforming to provided schemas. An external harness performs the actual execution (API calls, code runs) and returns observations, which the model conditions on to continue, enabling agentic loops of reasoning and acting.

questions.question: A ReAct-style agent interleaves reasoning traces with actions. The principal benefit of this interleaving is that:
options: It guarantees the agent never makes errors
It removes the need for any language model
Reasoning can inform which action to take next, and observations from actions can update subsequent reasoning
It eliminates the context window limit
questions.explanation: Interleaving lets each action be guided by current reasoning while observations feed back to refine later reasoning, forming a grounded loop.
questions.theory: ReAct frameworks alternate 'thought' (reasoning) and 'action' (tool call) steps, with the environment returning observations. This tight loop grounds reasoning in external feedback, helping the agent recover from mistakes and gather information rather than committing to a single, unverified plan.

questions.question: Why is evaluating open-ended LLM generation with a single automatic n-gram overlap metric (like BLEU or ROUGE) often inadequate?
options: These metrics require GPUs unavailable to most evaluators
They can only score classification tasks
They directly optimize the model weights during evaluation
They reward surface token overlap and can miss semantically correct answers that use different wording
questions.explanation: Overlap metrics penalize valid paraphrases and reward superficial matching, so they correlate weakly with human judgments of open-ended quality.
questions.theory: BLEU and ROUGE measure lexical overlap against references. For open-ended generation there are many acceptable outputs, so a correct response with different surface form scores poorly. This drives use of semantic, model-based, or human evaluation, each with its own limitations.

questions.question: Using a strong LLM as an automated judge to score other models' outputs introduces which characteristic risk?
options: Systematic biases, such as favoring longer responses or outputs stylistically similar to its own
It requires retraining the judged model each time
It is always slower than human evaluation
It cannot produce numeric scores
questions.explanation: LLM judges can exhibit biases like verbosity preference, position bias, and self-preference, skewing comparative scores.
questions.theory: LLM-as-a-judge offers scalable, cheap evaluation but inherits the judge's own biases. Documented issues include preferring longer or more confident answers, sensitivity to option ordering, and favoring outputs resembling its own style, so judge results require careful calibration and bias controls.

questions.question: 'Benchmark contamination' undermines reported LLM scores when:
options: Test set examples (or close variants) appeared in the model's training data, inflating measured performance
The benchmark is too small to be statistically meaningful
The benchmark questions are written by humans
The evaluation uses too many GPUs
questions.explanation: If test items leaked into pretraining data, the model may have effectively memorized answers, inflating scores beyond true generalization.
questions.theory: Web-scraped pretraining corpora can include public benchmark questions and solutions. When evaluation overlaps training data, high scores reflect memorization rather than capability, so contamination detection and held-out or freshly created tests are needed for trustworthy evaluation.

questions.question: In the context of LLM bias, why does training on large web corpora tend to propagate societal biases into the model?
options: Because tokenization randomly assigns biased labels
Because the optimizer deliberately injects bias to reduce loss
Because attention heads are initialized with biased priors
Because the model learns statistical regularities of its data, including stereotypical associations present in human-generated text
questions.explanation: Language models absorb correlations present in their training text, so biases and stereotypes embedded in that data are reflected in outputs.
questions.theory: Pretraining maximizes likelihood of human text, which encodes historical and societal biases. The model internalizes these statistical associations, potentially reproducing or amplifying them. Mitigation requires data curation, alignment techniques, and evaluation, none of which fully removes bias.

questions.question: Why is the term 'hallucination' used to describe a class of LLM errors, and what is the underlying cause?
options: The model intentionally lies to deceive users
The model generates fluent, confident text that is unsupported or false, because it optimizes next-token plausibility rather than truth
The retrieval index returns empty results
A hardware fault corrupts the output logits
questions.explanation: Hallucinations arise because the training objective rewards plausible continuations, not verified factual accuracy, so confident-yet-false output can result.
questions.theory: LLMs are trained to predict likely next tokens, producing text that is statistically coherent but not grounded in a truth model. When parametric knowledge is absent, stale, or conflated, the model can still emit confident, well-formed but incorrect statements, a key reliability limitation.

questions.question: Because next-token probabilities can be converted to a confidence score, a high-probability LLM answer is therefore guaranteed to be factually correct.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Token probability reflects modeled plausibility under the training distribution, not factual truth, so high confidence can accompany false answers.
questions.theory: Model confidence (e.g., token likelihood) measures how expected a continuation is, not its correctness. Calibration between confidence and accuracy is imperfect, and models can be confidently wrong, so probabilities are not a reliable standalone truth signal.

questions.question: Temperature and nucleus (top-p) sampling are decoding-time controls. Increasing temperature toward higher values primarily:
options: Disables the softmax entirely
Permanently changes the model's learned weights
Sharpens the distribution toward the single most likely token
Flattens the probability distribution, increasing randomness and diversity of sampled tokens
questions.explanation: Higher temperature scales logits down before softmax, flattening the distribution and increasing sampling randomness.
questions.theory: Temperature divides logits before the softmax; values above one flatten the distribution (more diverse, less predictable output) while values below one sharpen it (more deterministic). Top-p instead samples from the smallest token set whose cumulative probability exceeds p, truncating the long tail.

questions.question: A core limitation of fixed-context-window LLMs for long documents is that:
options: They cannot process any text longer than a single token
Longer context always improves accuracy linearly
Information beyond the context window is simply unavailable to the model unless it is retrieved or summarized into context
They automatically memorize all prior conversations forever
questions.explanation: Tokens outside the context window are not visible to the model, so long content must be chunked, summarized, or retrieved to be used.
questions.theory: A transformer attends only over tokens currently in its context window. Content exceeding that limit is invisible unless brought in via retrieval, summarization, or chunking. Even within the window, models can underutilize the middle of long contexts, a separate practical limitation.

questions.question: The 'lost in the middle' phenomenon in long-context LLMs describes the tendency to:
options: Forget the system prompt entirely
Crash when the context exceeds a fixed size
Utilize information placed at the beginning and end of a long context more effectively than information in the middle
Attend uniformly to all positions regardless of placement
questions.explanation: Models often retrieve relevant facts better when they appear near the start or end of a long context than when buried in the middle.
questions.theory: Empirical studies show a U-shaped performance curve over the position of relevant information in long contexts: recall is higher at the extremes and degrades in the middle. This has practical implications for ordering retrieved passages and prompt construction in long-context and RAG systems.

questions.question: In RAG, 'chunking' a corpus into passages before embedding involves a key tradeoff. Choosing very large chunks tends to:
options: Eliminate the need for an embedding model
Guarantee each chunk contains exactly one fact
Improve context completeness per chunk but dilute embedding specificity and waste context budget on irrelevant text
Always improve retrieval precision with no downside
questions.explanation: Large chunks carry more surrounding context but produce less focused embeddings and can fill the prompt with irrelevant material.
questions.theory: Chunk size mediates a tradeoff: small chunks yield specific embeddings and precise retrieval but may fragment context; large chunks preserve coherence but blur the embedding's semantic focus and consume more of the generator's limited context window, often reducing answer quality.

questions.question: Hybrid retrieval that combines dense (embedding) and sparse (e.g., BM25 keyword) methods is motivated because:
options: Combining them removes the need for any ranking
Dense retrieval captures semantic similarity while sparse retrieval excels at exact term and rare-keyword matching, and combining them improves robustness
Dense retrieval cannot run on text data
Sparse methods are always strictly superior to dense ones
questions.explanation: Dense and sparse retrieval have complementary strengths, so hybrids improve robustness across semantic and exact-match queries.
questions.theory: Sparse lexical methods like BM25 reliably match exact terms, rare tokens, and identifiers but miss paraphrases. Dense retrieval captures semantics but can overlook precise keywords. Hybrid systems fuse both signals (e.g., score combination) to handle a wider range of query types.

questions.question: Catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning of an LLM refers to:
options: The model deleting its tokenizer
Loss of previously acquired capabilities as new-task training overwrites parameters important for the old distribution
The training data being permanently erased from disk
An increase in context window size
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning on a narrow new task can overwrite weights encoding prior knowledge, degrading earlier capabilities.
questions.theory: When a network is trained sequentially on new data without rehearsal, gradient updates can shift parameters away from configurations that supported earlier tasks, eroding prior performance. Techniques like parameter-efficient tuning, regularization, or data mixing help mitigate this.

questions.question: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as low-rank adaptation (LoRA) reduce cost by:
options: Retraining all weights at full precision every step
Freezing most pretrained weights and learning small low-rank update matrices injected into the network
Removing the attention layers entirely
Replacing fine-tuning with prompt injection
questions.explanation: LoRA keeps base weights frozen and trains compact low-rank matrices, drastically cutting the number of trainable parameters.
questions.theory: Low-rank adaptation hypothesizes that task-specific weight changes lie in a low-dimensional subspace. By inserting trainable low-rank matrices while freezing the original weights, it achieves adaptation with far fewer trainable parameters and memory, often approaching full fine-tuning quality.

questions.question: Why can two different LLMs report very different token counts for the same input string?
options: One model ignores whitespace entirely
Token counts depend only on the number of characters
They use different tokenizers (vocabularies and subword segmentation rules), so the same text splits into different numbers of tokens
They use different floating-point precisions for embeddings
questions.explanation: Tokenization depends on the model's specific subword vocabulary and merge rules, so identical text can yield different token counts across models.
questions.theory: LLMs operate on tokens defined by a learned subword tokenizer (e.g., byte-pair encoding). Different vocabularies and merge rules segment text differently, affecting token counts, context-window consumption, and cost, even though the underlying characters are identical.

questions.question: A pretrained base language model, before any instruction tuning or alignment, is inherently optimized to follow user instructions and refuse harmful requests.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: A raw pretrained model only predicts likely continuations; instruction-following and safety behaviors come from later tuning and alignment.
questions.theory: Base models are trained purely for next-token prediction on large corpora, so they continue text rather than obey instructions or apply safety norms. Instruction tuning and alignment (e.g., supervised fine-tuning plus RLHF) are what shape helpful, harmless, instruction-following behavior.

questions.question: Instruction tuning (supervised fine-tuning on instruction-response pairs) primarily changes a base model by:
options: Teaching it to map natural-language instructions to appropriately formatted, task-following responses
Converting it from a decoder to an encoder
Increasing its vocabulary size
Removing its ability to generate text
questions.explanation: Instruction tuning fits the model to respond to instructions in the desired format, bridging raw prediction and helpful task completion.
questions.theory: Supervised instruction tuning trains the model on curated instruction-response examples, shifting its conditional distribution toward producing helpful, well-formatted answers when prompted with instructions. It typically precedes preference-based alignment and is key to usable assistant behavior.

questions.question: A 'jailbreak' prompt against an aligned LLM aims to:
options: Improve the model's factual accuracy
Compress the model into a smaller file
Elicit responses that violate the model's safety policies by circumventing its alignment guardrails
Increase the model's training speed
questions.explanation: Jailbreaks craft inputs that bypass safety constraints to obtain disallowed outputs from an otherwise aligned model.
questions.theory: Alignment installs refusal and safety behaviors, but these are learned tendencies rather than hard guarantees. Jailbreaks exploit gaps via role-play framing, obfuscation, or adversarial phrasing to push the model out of its safe behavior distribution, an ongoing robustness challenge.

questions.question: Why does granting an LLM agent broad tool permissions (e.g., file deletion, payments) amplify the impact of prompt injection?
options: It reduces the model's token count
It widens the action space an attacker can trigger, so a successful injection can cause real-world side effects, not just bad text
It makes the model run faster
It eliminates the need for a system prompt
questions.explanation: With powerful tools, a hijacked agent can execute consequential actions, turning a text-level exploit into real-world harm.
questions.theory: Agent risk scales with capability: when an injected instruction can invoke high-privilege tools, the blast radius extends beyond misleading text to data loss, unauthorized transactions, or exfiltration. The principle of least privilege and human-in-the-loop confirmation are central mitigations.

questions.question: Self-consistency decoding improves reasoning accuracy by:
options: Removing all intermediate reasoning tokens
Lowering temperature to zero for a single greedy answer
Retraining the model after each query
Sampling multiple chain-of-thought paths and selecting the most frequent final answer
questions.explanation: Self-consistency aggregates several sampled reasoning paths and takes a majority vote over their final answers, improving robustness.
questions.theory: Because different sampled reasoning chains may reach the same correct conclusion through varied paths, marginalizing over diverse chains and voting on the final answer reduces the impact of any single faulty trace, typically boosting accuracy on reasoning tasks at extra inference cost.

questions.question: Retrieval in RAG decouples knowledge from parameters. A direct practical advantage of this is that:
options: Embeddings no longer need to be computed
The model becomes immune to prompt injection
The model never needs a context window
Knowledge can be updated by changing the external corpus without retraining the model weights
questions.explanation: Because knowledge lives in an external store, updating documents refreshes what the system can answer without modifying model weights.
questions.theory: RAG separates a relatively static parametric model from a mutable external knowledge base. Updating, adding, or removing documents changes available knowledge instantly and cheaply, addressing staleness and enabling domain customization without the cost and risk of retraining.

questions.question: When a RAG system retrieves a passage that contradicts the model's parametric knowledge, the desired behavior is usually to:
options: Randomly choose between the two sources
Refuse to answer in all cases
Ground the answer in the authoritative retrieved evidence, since it is typically more current or task-specific than parametric memory
Always ignore the retrieved passage and trust memorized parameters
questions.explanation: RAG is designed so the model defers to provided, presumably authoritative context rather than potentially stale parametric memory.
questions.theory: A central purpose of RAG is to override outdated or uncertain parametric knowledge with curated, current external evidence. Faithfulness to retrieved context is generally preferred, though this assumes the retrieval is trustworthy, highlighting the importance of source quality and conflict handling.

questions.question: In RAG evaluation, 'faithfulness' (or groundedness) specifically measures whether:
options: The user found the answer helpful overall
The retrieved documents are ranked in the correct order
The embedding model is the largest available
The generated answer's claims are actually supported by the retrieved context rather than fabricated
questions.explanation: Faithfulness checks that the answer's assertions are entailed by the retrieved evidence rather than hallucinated.
questions.theory: RAG quality decomposes into retrieval metrics (did we fetch the right context) and generation metrics like faithfulness (does the answer stick to that context) and answer relevance. A faithful answer makes only claims grounded in provided evidence, reducing hallucination even when retrieval succeeds.

questions.question: Sycophancy in aligned LLMs, where the model tends to agree with a user's stated view even when it is wrong, is often attributed to:
options: Preference data and optimization that reward agreeable, approval-seeking responses, so the model learns to tell users what they want to hear
Insufficient context window length
A bug in the tokenizer
The quadratic cost of attention
questions.explanation: Human preference signals can favor agreeable answers, so alignment optimization can inadvertently train the model toward sycophancy.
questions.theory: If human raters reward responses that affirm their beliefs, the reward model encodes that preference, and the policy learns to be agreeable rather than accurate. Sycophancy illustrates how optimizing for human approval can diverge from optimizing for truthfulness, a known alignment pitfall.

questions.question: Why is reliable evaluation of an LLM's reasoning especially difficult compared to evaluating a narrow classifier?
options: Reasoning can be measured perfectly with accuracy alone
Outputs are open-ended and a model may reach a right answer via flawed reasoning (or vice versa), so scoring requires assessing process and answer, not just a label
Reasoning tasks have no possible correct answers
Classifiers cannot be evaluated at all
questions.explanation: Open-ended reasoning can decouple correct answers from sound process, so a single accuracy label fails to capture true capability.
questions.theory: Unlike fixed-label classification, generative reasoning produces free-form outputs where correct conclusions may follow invalid steps or correct steps may yield wrong answers. Trustworthy evaluation must consider both the final answer and the validity of the reasoning, complicating automatic scoring.

questions.question: Positional encodings are added to transformer token embeddings because:
options: They reduce the model's parameter count
They replace the need for a vocabulary
They perform the final softmax over tokens
Self-attention is otherwise order-agnostic, so explicit position information is needed to represent sequence order
questions.explanation: Self-attention treats inputs as an unordered set, so positional encodings inject the order information required for language.
questions.theory: The attention operation itself is permutation-equivariant and carries no notion of token order. Positional encodings (absolute, learned, or relative schemes such as rotary embeddings) supply order information so the model can distinguish sequences that differ only in arrangement, which is essential for language.

questions.question: A defense-in-depth strategy for an LLM application processing untrusted input would LEAST rely on which single measure as a complete safeguard?
options: Isolating untrusted data from privileged instructions and adding human review for high-risk actions
Restricting tool permissions to the minimum necessary
Validating and constraining model outputs before acting on them
A single cleverly worded system prompt instructing the model to be safe
questions.explanation: A lone system prompt is a weak, bypassable control, so robust security layers multiple independent defenses rather than relying on it alone.
questions.theory: LLM safety follows defense-in-depth: least-privilege tool scoping, input/output validation, provenance separation, and human oversight together reduce risk. Because models offer no hard guarantees, no single prompt-level instruction should be treated as a complete safeguard against adversarial input.

questions.question: You want a model to summarize an article. Which prompt is most likely to get the result you want?
options: Article.
Summarize this article in 3 bullet points, each under 15 words.
Make this better.
Do something with this article.
questions.explanation: Specifying the task, format, and length gives the model clear, actionable instructions.
questions.theory: Clear and specific instructions reduce ambiguity about what you want. The more precisely you state the task, format, and constraints, the more likely the output matches your intent.

questions.question: Giving the model relevant background context generally helps it produce a more useful answer.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Context helps the model tailor its response to your specific situation instead of guessing.
questions.theory: Models respond only to the information in the prompt. Supplying relevant context lets the model ground its answer in your actual situation rather than generic assumptions.

questions.question: Which prompt best specifies the desired output format?
options: Tell me about the causes.
List the top 3 causes as a numbered list.
I'm curious about causes.
Causes?
questions.explanation: Asking for a numbered list of exactly three items defines the format clearly.
questions.theory: Stating the format you want (list, table, paragraph, JSON) tells the model how to structure its response. Without it, the model picks a format that may not suit your needs.

questions.question: You need a polite email declining a meeting. Which prompt gives the model enough to work with?
options: Write something.
Decline.
Email.
Write a short, polite email declining a meeting because I have a scheduling conflict.
questions.explanation: It states the task, tone, length, and reason, so the model can produce a fitting email.
questions.theory: A good prompt names the task and includes the key details the model needs, such as tone, length, and reason. Missing details force the model to guess.

questions.question: Why is 'Make it good' usually a weak instruction?
options: It uses too many technical terms.
Models cannot understand the word 'good'.
It is too long for the model to read.
It is vague and does not define what 'good' means for the task.
questions.explanation: Without criteria for 'good', the model has no clear target to aim for.
questions.theory: Vague quality words like 'good' or 'better' mean different things in different contexts. Naming concrete criteria (shorter, friendlier, more formal) gives the model a measurable target.

questions.question: Which prompt is most likely to produce output you can paste directly into a spreadsheet?
options: Give me the data nicely.
Show the data.
Return the data as comma-separated values with columns Name, Age, City.
Put the data somewhere useful.
questions.explanation: Specifying CSV with named columns defines an exact, machine-readable format.
questions.theory: When you need output in a specific structure, describe that structure explicitly. Naming the format and the fields lets the model produce something ready to use.

questions.question: Including one example of the output you want can help the model match your desired style.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: An example shows the model the pattern to follow, which words alone may not convey.
questions.theory: Showing an example of the desired output demonstrates format and style directly. This often communicates expectations more reliably than describing them in words.

questions.question: You want the model to act as a friendly customer-support agent. What is the best way to set this up?
options: Tell the model to respond as a friendly customer-support agent.
Type the question in all capital letters.
Repeat the question several times.
Hope the model guesses the right tone.
questions.explanation: Stating the desired role and tone directs the model toward the style you need.
questions.theory: Describing the role or persona you want the model to adopt shapes its tone and word choice. Explicit role instructions are more reliable than hoping for a default style.

questions.question: Which addition would most improve the prompt 'Translate this'?
options: Ask the model if it is ready first.
Write it in bold.
Specify the target language, such as 'Translate this into Spanish'.
Add three exclamation points.
questions.explanation: Naming the target language removes a key ambiguity in the translation task.
questions.theory: Identify and fill in the missing details a task requires. For translation, the target language is essential information the model cannot infer reliably.

questions.question: You ask for a recipe but get one with peanuts, which you are allergic to. How should you have prompted?
options: State your constraint up front: 'Suggest a recipe with no peanuts.'
Tell the model to try harder.
Ask for the recipe in a different font.
Ask the same question again unchanged.
questions.explanation: Stating constraints in the prompt lets the model honor them from the start.
questions.theory: Constraints and requirements should be stated explicitly in the prompt. The model cannot account for needs it was never told about, such as allergies or restrictions.

questions.question: Which prompt best controls the length of the response?
options: Explain photosynthesis in about two sentences.
Explain photosynthesis.
Tell me everything you can.
Photosynthesis info please.
questions.explanation: Specifying 'about two sentences' sets a clear length expectation.
questions.theory: Stating a target length (word count, sentence count, or bullet count) controls how much detail the model gives. Without it, response length is unpredictable.

questions.question: You want a definition a 10-year-old can understand. Which prompt is best?
options: Explain what gravity is in simple terms a 10-year-old would understand.
Explain gravity.
Gravity.
Define gravity using physics equations.
questions.explanation: Naming the audience and reading level guides the model's vocabulary and depth.
questions.theory: Telling the model who the audience is helps it choose appropriate vocabulary and depth. Audience cues are a simple way to shape tone and complexity.

questions.question: A prompt that asks for two unrelated things at once can produce a less focused answer than asking one thing at a time.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Combining unrelated requests can split the model's attention and dilute each answer.
questions.theory: Focused prompts tend to yield focused answers. When tasks are unrelated, separating them into distinct prompts often produces clearer results for each.

questions.question: Which prompt gives the model the context it needs to write a relevant product description?
options: Describe it.
Write a product description.
Write a product description for a waterproof hiking backpack aimed at beginners.
Backpack words.
questions.explanation: Naming the product, its key feature, and the audience gives the model what it needs.
questions.theory: Useful context includes what the subject is, its notable features, and who it's for. The more relevant context you supply, the more on-target the output.

questions.question: You want exactly five tips, no more and no less. Which prompt enforces this best?
options: Give me some tips for saving money.
Help me save money somehow.
Tips for groceries.
Give me exactly 5 tips for saving money on groceries.
questions.explanation: Stating 'exactly 5' sets a precise count the model can follow.
questions.theory: When you need a specific quantity, say it explicitly. Words like 'some' or 'a few' leave the count up to the model.

questions.question: Which is the clearest way to ask the model to fix only grammar, not style?
options: Fix this.
Improve this however you like.
Make this text good.
Correct only the grammar in this text and leave the wording unchanged.
questions.explanation: It scopes the task to grammar and explicitly excludes other changes.
questions.theory: Defining the scope of a task tells the model what to do and what to avoid. Explicit boundaries prevent unwanted changes.

questions.question: Your first answer was too formal. What is the best follow-up prompt?
options: Try again.
Different.
No.
Rewrite that in a more casual, conversational tone.
questions.explanation: It states exactly what to change, giving the model a clear direction.
questions.theory: When refining an answer, name the specific change you want. Concrete feedback like 'more casual' guides revision better than vague disapproval.

questions.question: Telling the model what to do is usually clearer than only telling it what not to do.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Positive instructions describe the target directly instead of leaving many options open.
questions.theory: Positive instructions point to the desired outcome, while negative-only instructions leave many alternatives undefined. Stating what you want is typically more effective.

questions.question: Which prompt best uses an example to guide formatting?
options: Make the list look good.
Format my list nicely.
Format each entry like this example: 'Name - Role - City'. Now format my list.
Do the list.
questions.explanation: Providing a concrete pattern shows the model exactly how to format each entry.
questions.theory: An example pattern demonstrates the structure you want each item to follow. This is often clearer than describing the format abstractly.

questions.question: You want the model to write code only in Python. What should the prompt include?
options: A demand for the longest answer possible.
No mention of language at all.
A request to surprise you with any language.
An explicit statement: 'Write the solution in Python.'
questions.explanation: Naming the language removes ambiguity about which language to use.
questions.theory: When a task has a key parameter like programming language, state it explicitly. The model otherwise chooses a default that may not match your needs.

questions.question: Which prompt provides the context needed for a useful answer about a specific error?
options: My code is broken, fix it.
Why doesn't it work?
Here is my code and the exact error message it produces; explain what causes it.
Something is wrong somewhere.
questions.explanation: Including the code and the exact error gives the model the details it needs to diagnose.
questions.theory: Supplying the relevant artifacts (code, error text, inputs) gives the model concrete material to reason about. Vague descriptions force guesswork.

questions.question: You ask 'Write a story' and dislike the result. Which revision would help most?
options: Write a story but better.
Try once more.
Story again.
Write a 200-word adventure story for kids set in a forest.
questions.explanation: Adding genre, length, audience, and setting gives the model concrete direction.
questions.theory: Specifying attributes like length, genre, audience, and setting narrows the space of acceptable outputs. More detail yields results closer to your intent.

questions.question: If the model needs to know who the reader is, including the audience in the prompt helps it choose an appropriate tone.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Audience information lets the model match vocabulary and formality to the reader.
questions.theory: The intended audience affects tone, vocabulary, and detail level. Telling the model who will read the output helps it write appropriately for them.

questions.question: Which prompt most clearly defines the task you want performed?
options: Reviews.
Do the review thing.
Classify each review below as positive, negative, or neutral.
Look at these reviews.
questions.explanation: It names the action (classify) and the exact categories to use.
questions.theory: A clear task statement names the action to perform and any categories or rules involved. This leaves little doubt about what output is expected.

questions.question: You want a table comparing two phones. Which prompt best specifies the format?
options: Compare phone A and phone B in a table with rows for price, battery, and camera.
Which phone?
Phones, go.
Compare the phones.
questions.explanation: It requests a table and names the specific rows to include.
questions.theory: When you want a comparison in a particular layout, specify the format and the dimensions to compare. This produces a structured, easy-to-read result.

questions.question: Which prompt gives the model the clearest goal?
options: Change this paragraph.
Make this paragraph different somehow.
Rewrite this paragraph to be more concise without losing key facts.
Edit.
questions.explanation: It states the goal (concise) and the constraint (keep key facts).
questions.theory: Stating both the goal and any constraints tells the model what to optimize for and what to preserve. This focuses the edit on your real intent.

questions.question: You want the answer to start with a one-sentence summary. How do you ensure that?
options: Put the request in invisible text.
Ask in a language the model doesn't use.
Ask explicitly: 'Begin with a one-sentence summary, then give details.'
Assume the model will do it automatically.
questions.explanation: Explicitly describing the desired structure makes the model follow it.
questions.theory: If you want a particular structure or ordering, describe it directly. Models follow explicit structural instructions more reliably than implied ones.

questions.question: Which prompt would most reduce the chance of an off-topic answer?
options: Answer only about renewable energy and ignore unrelated topics.
Tell me about stuff.
Whatever comes to mind.
Talk about anything related.
questions.explanation: Restricting the topic keeps the response focused on what you care about.
questions.theory: Scoping the topic with explicit boundaries keeps responses on target. Open-ended prompts invite tangents that may not be useful.

questions.question: Pasting the relevant document into the prompt lets the model answer questions about that document.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: The model can only use information it is given, so including the document enables grounded answers.
questions.theory: Models work from the text in the prompt. Providing the source document directly gives the model the material it needs to answer accurately about it.

questions.question: Which prompt best combines a clear task with a clear format?
options: Report summary thing.
Summarize this report in 5 bullet points for executives.
Make the report shorter or longer.
Handle this report.
questions.explanation: It states the task (summarize), the format (5 bullets), and the audience (executives).
questions.theory: Strong prompts often pair a clear task with a clear format and audience. Combining these elements makes the expected output unambiguous.

questions.question: You want consistent formatting across many items. What technique helps most?
options: Give no guidance and hope items match.
Show one fully formatted example, then ask the model to follow it.
Ask for each item in a random order.
Tell the model to be creative with each item.
questions.explanation: A worked example sets a template the model can apply uniformly.
questions.theory: A single clear example acts as a template the model imitates. This is an easy way to get consistent structure across many outputs.

questions.question: Which prompt is most specific about what to extract?
options: Find what matters here.
Get the info.
Extract the names of all people mentioned in this text.
Pull out the important parts.
questions.explanation: It names exactly what to extract: people's names.
questions.theory: Specify exactly what information you want extracted. Naming the target (dates, names, prices) is far clearer than asking for 'the important parts'.

questions.question: You keep getting answers that are too long. What is the best fix?
options: Add more exclamation marks.
Add a length limit, such as 'Answer in no more than 50 words.'
Ask the same way but louder with capitals.
Repeat the question twice.
questions.explanation: An explicit length limit directly constrains the response size.
questions.theory: To control verbosity, give an explicit length constraint. A stated word or sentence limit is the most direct way to shorten outputs.

questions.question: Which prompt provides the clearest instruction for tone?
options: Write this announcement.
Announcement.
Write this announcement in an upbeat, enthusiastic tone.
Do an announcement, you decide how.
questions.explanation: Naming the tone (upbeat, enthusiastic) tells the model the feel to convey.
questions.theory: Tone words like formal, friendly, or enthusiastic steer the emotional style of the output. Stating the tone you want makes the result more predictable.

questions.question: You want the model to return its answer as JSON. Which prompt is best?
options: Return the answer as JSON with keys 'title' and 'summary'.
Format however looks nice.
Give me the answer in some structured way.
Make it organized.
questions.explanation: It names the format (JSON) and the exact keys to include.
questions.theory: For machine-readable output, name the exact format and the required fields. Precise structure requests yield output that downstream tools can parse.

questions.question: Which prompt best clarifies an ambiguous request like 'Tell me about Java'?
options: Explain Java, your choice of meaning.
Tell me about the Java programming language, focusing on what it's used for.
Tell me about Java.
Java?
questions.explanation: It resolves the ambiguity by specifying the programming language and the focus.
questions.theory: When a term has multiple meanings, disambiguate it in the prompt. Adding context steers the model to the interpretation you intend.

questions.question: Breaking a vague request into specific requirements usually makes the model's output more useful.
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Specific requirements give the model clear targets instead of leaving it to guess.
questions.theory: Decomposing a fuzzy goal into concrete requirements clarifies intent. Each added specification narrows the output toward what you actually need.

questions.question: Which prompt gives the model the best context to write a cover letter?
options: Write me a cover letter.
Here is the job description and my resume; write a cover letter tailored to this role.
Cover letter please.
Make a letter for a job.
questions.explanation: Providing the job description and resume lets the model tailor the letter accurately.
questions.theory: Tailored outputs require the relevant inputs. Supplying the specific materials lets the model produce something fitted to your situation rather than generic.

questions.question: You want bullet points, but the model keeps writing paragraphs. What should you do?
options: Explicitly request bullet points in your prompt.
Assume it will switch on its own.
Wait and ask the same question later.
Add more blank lines to the prompt.
questions.explanation: Stating the format you want directs the model to use it.
questions.theory: If the default format isn't what you want, state your preferred format explicitly. The model follows clear formatting instructions when they are given.

questions.question: Which prompt most clearly tells the model the desired point of view?
options: Write this paragraph.
Do a paragraph.
Write this paragraph in the first person, as if I am speaking.
Paragraph, any style.
questions.explanation: It specifies first-person point of view, removing that ambiguity.
questions.theory: Point of view (first, second, or third person) shapes how text reads. Specifying it directly ensures the output uses the perspective you want.

questions.question: Which prompt best prevents the model from inventing details you did not provide?
options: Answer using whatever you think is right.
Fill in any gaps with your best guess.
Make the answer sound complete no matter what.
Answer using only the information in the text above; if it's not there, say so.
questions.explanation: Restricting the model to the given text and allowing 'not stated' reduces made-up details.
questions.theory: Instructing the model to rely only on provided information, and to admit when something is missing, helps keep answers grounded and reduces fabrication.

questions.question: Which is the better prompt for getting step-by-step instructions?
options: Printer.
List the steps to set up the printer, numbered in order.
Tell me about the printer setup.
Setup info.
questions.explanation: Asking for numbered steps in order specifies a clear, sequential format.
questions.theory: When you need a procedure, ask for ordered, numbered steps. Specifying the sequential format produces instructions that are easy to follow.

questions.question: You want an answer suitable for a formal business report. Which prompt is best?
options: Write this however.
Make it sound okay.
Write this in a formal, professional tone appropriate for a business report.
Write this casually with slang.
questions.explanation: It names the tone and the context, guiding the model to the right register.
questions.theory: Matching tone to context matters. Telling the model the setting (business report) and tone (formal) yields output that fits where it will be used.

questions.question: Which prompt best demonstrates 'show, don't just tell' for the output you want?
options: Do Q&A.
Write some questions and answers.
Here is a sample question and answer in the style I want; now write three more like it.
Make questions, any style is fine.
questions.explanation: Providing a sample and asking for more in that style gives the model a concrete pattern.
